studyMetadata.title: Working for the Good
studyMetadata.subtitle: Faith, Love, and Worship in our Everyday Labour
studyMetadata.description: An eight-session Bible study exploring what it means to work faithfully as a Christian. Drawing on passages from Genesis to Revelation, each session tackles a different dimension of work: its God-given goodness, its distortion by sin, and its redemption in Christ. Whether you feel your job is meaningless, disconnected from your faith, or simply exhausting, this study invites you to see your daily labour as an act of love, a form of worship, and part of God’s larger purposes for the world.
studyMetadata.author: Rick Creighton
studyMetadata.shareMessage:
Check out this Bible study app — “Working for the Good: Faith, Love, and Worship in our Everyday Labour”.

Available soon on Google Play and the App Store.

theme.theme-annotation: Warm labour theme — earthy parchment and terracotta palette. Pulled from the tones of worn wood, dusty stone, and deep rust. Grounded and dignified, evoking ordinary work as something weighty and good. Suited to studies on vocation, creation, and everyday faithfulness.

elements.text:
David is a financial officer in a medium-sized law firm. He’s worked hard, achieved career success, and serves as a church leader. But lately, he’s been restless. He says:
’I feel like my job doesn’t count for Jesus. I try to share the gospel at work when I can, but beyond that, it just feels like I’m wasting my life. I know I’ve been successful, but I don’t feel like what I’m doing at work is significant.’
David is wondering if he should leave his job and move into full-time Christian work, or perhaps work for a charity. He wants his life to mean something for Jesus.

elements.question: When have you felt most satisfied in your work? Why?
When have you felt your work was meaningless or disconnected from your faith?
What are some messages our culture gives us about what makes work ’valuable’ or ’important’?

elements.text: God created work from the very beginning. It’s part of his purposes for creation.

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>The Lord God took the man and placed him in the orchard in Eden to care for it and to maintain it.

elements.question: What can we learn about God’s purpose for work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Work was given by God before the Fall — it was part of his original good design for humanity, not a consequence of sin. God placed Adam in the garden with a specific task: to care for and maintain it. This tells us that work is a gift and a calling, not a curse. God’s purpose for work is that human beings would tend, cultivate, and order the world he made — participating in his ongoing care for creation.
elements.questionHint: Notice that this verse comes before the Fall — what does that tell you about whether work itself is part of God’s good plan or a punishment?

elements.question: How might this change the way we think about ’ordinary’ jobs?
elements.sampleAnswer: If work was designed by God as a way of caring for and maintaining his creation, then no job that contributes to human flourishing is truly ’ordinary’. A cleaner maintains order and dignity; a farmer tends creation; an accountant helps organisations serve others well. The categories of ’spiritual’ versus ’secular’ work start to break down when we see all work as participation in God’s purposes for the world.
elements.questionHint: If gardening and animal care were noble enough for Eden, how might that reframe the kinds of work we tend to dismiss as unimportant?

elements.question: What do you think work would have felt like before the Fall?
elements.sampleAnswer: Work before the Fall would have been fulfilling without being frustrating. There would have been no futility, no thorns, no broken relationships with colleagues, no systems of injustice distorting the fruit of labour. Adam’s work in the garden aligned perfectly with his nature and God’s purposes — it would have felt creative, purposeful, and satisfying in a deep and lasting way. The work itself would have felt like worship.
elements.questionHint: Think about what is missing from work in Genesis 2 that appears in Genesis 3 — what does that absence tell you about what work was originally like?

elements.text: After Adam and Eve sinned, work became hard and painful. It’s still part of our calling, but now it is marked by toil, frustration, and thorns. Work is still good, but it no longer always feels good.

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>But to Adam he said, "Because you obeyed your wife and ate from the tree about which I commanded you, ‘You must not eat from it,‘ cursed is the ground thanks to you; in painful toil you will eat of it all the days of your life. <sup>18</sup>It will produce thorns and thistles for you, but you will eat the grain of the field. <sup>19</sup>By the sweat of your brow you will eat food until you return to the ground, for out of it you were taken; for you are dust, and to dust you will return."

elements.question: How do we see the frustration of work?
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage introduces three key sources of frustration: resistance (thorns and thistles fighting against our efforts), toil (work now requires painful effort rather than joyful engagement), and futility (we return to dust, raising questions about whether our efforts outlast us). We see this everywhere today — projects that stall, colleagues who undermine, systems that grind people down, the exhaustion of giving everything to a job and feeling it is never enough.
elements.questionHint: What specific words or images in verses 17–19 describe what has changed about work — and can you match them to experiences you recognise from your own working life?

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>I expanded my pursuits. I built houses for myself; I planted vineyards for myself. <sup>5</sup>I made gardens and parks for myself, and I planted all kinds of fruit trees in them. <sup>6</sup>I made pools of water for myself, to irrigate a grove of flourishing trees. <sup>7</sup>I acquired male and female slaves, and I owned slaves who were born in my house; I also had great herds of cattle and flocks of sheep, more than any of my predecessors in Jerusalem. <sup>8</sup>I also amassed silver and gold for myself, the treasure of kings and provinces. I acquired male singers and female singers for myself, and what gives a man sensual delight – a harem of beautiful concubines. <sup>9</sup>So I was far greater than all my predecessors in Jerusalem; yet my wisdom remained with me. <sup>10</sup>Nothing that I desired did I fail to give myself – I did not refuse myself any pleasure – for I took pleasure in all my toil, and this pleasure was my reward for all my toil. <sup>11</sup>Yet when I reflected on everything I had accomplished and what I had toiled to achieve, I concluded: "All these achievements and possessions are profitless – like chasing the wind! There is nothing of real value in them!" <sup>17</sup>So I loathed life because what happens on earth seems awful to me; for all the benefits of wisdom result in nothing – it is all like chasing the wind. <sup>18</sup>So I loathed all the fruits of my labor for which I worked so hard on earth, because I must leave them to the person who comes after me. <sup>19</sup>Who knows if he will be a wise man or a fool? Yet he will be master over all the fruit of my labor for which I worked so wisely on earth! This also is futile! <sup>20</sup>So I began to despair about all the fruit of my labor for which I worked so hard on earth. <sup>21</sup>For a person may do his work with wisdom, knowledge, and skill; however, he must hand over the fruit of his labor as an inheritance to someone else who did not work for it. This also is futile, and an awful injustice! <sup>22</sup>What does a person acquire from all his labor and from the anxiety that accompanies his toil on earth? <sup>23</sup>For all day long his work produces pain and frustration, and even at night his mind cannot relax! This also is futile!

elements.question: What can make work seem fleeting or meaningless?
elements.sampleAnswer: Qohelet identifies several things that drain work of lasting meaning: building and achieving for oneself rather than for others or God; the knowledge that everything we build must eventually be handed to someone else who did not earn it; the anxiety that never switches off even at night; and the gap between effort and reward — working wisely and hard, only for the fruits to pass to a fool. Work becomes meaningless when it is disconnected from anything greater than personal accumulation or legacy.
elements.questionHint: Notice how many times the word ’myself’ appears in verses 4–8 — what does that repeated word suggest about why the Teacher’s work ultimately felt empty?

elements.question: How do you experience the ’thorns and thistles’ of work?
elements.sampleAnswer: The thorns and thistles of work show up in the anxiety that follows us home, the sense that our best efforts may be undone by others, the frustration of injustice in the workplace, and the exhaustion of never being able to fully rest. For me, they appear in the gap between the work I pour myself into and the recognition or fruit I actually see — and in the way work can colonise sleep and rest, leaving the mind unable to switch off.
elements.questionHint: Verse 23 describes a specific experience of work bleeding into night-time — does that resonate with anything in your own life, and what does it tell you about what work has become since the Fall?

elements.text: Because of Jesus, work is no longer meaningless. Christ redeems not only us as workers but our work itself. Our work does not save us, but when done in faith and love, it becomes an offering of worship.

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>Whatever you are doing, work at it with enthusiasm, as to the Lord and not for people, <sup>24</sup>because you know that you will receive your inheritance from the Lord as the reward. Serve the Lord Christ.

elements.question: How does knowing Jesus change how we see work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul reframes the entire audience for our work: instead of working for the approval of a manager, a client, or even our own sense of achievement, we work ’as to the Lord’. This transforms even the most routine task — it has a new recipient. The promise of an inheritance from Christ also gives work a future dimension: our labour is not lost, even when it goes unrecognised by people. Knowing Jesus means our work has an ultimate audience, an eternal reward, and a daily dignity that no employer can grant or remove.
elements.questionHint: Paul says to work ’as to the Lord and not for people’ — how might changing who you are ultimately working for change the way a difficult or unrewarding task feels?

elements.passageText: <sup>31</sup>So whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do everything for the glory of God.

elements.question: What kinds of work might seem insignificant, but are deeply valuable when done for Jesus?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s ’whatever you do’ is deliberately all-encompassing — even eating and drinking are included. So work that seems too small to matter — washing dishes, filing paperwork, answering routine emails, picking up litter, caring for an elderly relative — becomes an act of worship when offered to God. The value of the work is not determined by its visibility or social prestige, but by the heart and purpose with which it is done.
elements.questionHint: Paul includes even eating and drinking in his ’whatever you do’ — what does that suggest about whether any legitimate activity is too small to be done for God’s glory?

elements.question: How can you make your workplace a place of worship this week?
elements.sampleAnswer: Worship in the workplace doesn’t require a song or a sermon. It might look like doing careful, honest work that honours God even when no one is watching; treating a difficult colleague with patience and dignity; refusing to cut corners; bringing creativity and diligence to a task as an act of thanks to God. This week, I could begin each morning by consciously offering the day’s work to God — turning the commute or the first task into a moment of prayer and intention.
elements.questionHint: If doing everything ’for the glory of God’ is the standard, what is one specific thing you could do differently at work this week that would more clearly reflect that aim?

elements.text: Our worth is not measured by our job title, or income. It’s grounded in God’s grace. We don’t work to become someone; we work because of who we already are in Christ.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>For by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God; <sup>9</sup>it is not from works, so that no one can boast. <sup>10</sup>For we are his workmanship, having been created in Christ Jesus for good works that God prepared beforehand so we may do them.

elements.question: How does our work follow from God’s work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul makes a striking sequence: God first works on us (saving us by grace, making us his workmanship), and then we work for him (the good works he prepared in advance). Our work is therefore a response to and continuation of God’s own prior work. We don’t work to earn our place; we work because our place is already secured. God’s creative and redemptive work in us spills out into creative and redemptive work in the world. Our labour is downstream of grace.
elements.questionHint: Paul says we are God’s ’workmanship’ created for ’good works’ — how does the order of those two things (what God does first, then what we do) shape the way we should understand our motivation for working?

elements.question: How might strengthening your grasp on your identity in Christ bring freedom and joy to your daily work?
elements.sampleAnswer: If my identity rests on my performance at work, then a bad day, a missed promotion, or a failed project threatens who I am. But if I am already God’s workmanship — made and loved in Christ — then my work flows from security rather than anxiety. I don’t need my job to justify me. This frees me to take risks, serve others generously, do unglamorous work without resentment, and handle failure without despair. The deeper my grasp of grace, the lighter the burden of needing work to make me someone.
elements.questionHint: If verse 9 rules out boasting in our works, and verse 10 says good works were prepared for us in advance, what does that suggest about the difference between working to earn worth versus working from a place of already being loved?

elements.text: Work is one of the main ways we love others. Through our jobs, homes, and communities, we contribute to human flourishing. We love many people in a few ways (through our work) and a few people in many ways (through relationships).

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Now on the topic of brotherly love you have no need for anyone to write you, for you yourselves are taught by God to love one another. <sup>10</sup>And indeed you are practicing it toward all the brothers and sisters in all of Macedonia. But we urge you, brothers and sisters, to do so more and more, <sup>11</sup>to aspire to lead a quiet life, to attend to your own business, and to work with your hands, as we commanded you. <sup>12</sup>In this way you will live a decent life before outsiders and not be in need.

elements.question: What are some of the positives that come from faithful work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul identifies several fruits of faithful work: self-sufficiency (not being in need), a good witness before outsiders, and participation in the broader community of love. Faithful work produces dignity — both for the worker and for those who receive the work’s fruit. It also creates credibility: a Christian who works honestly and diligently commends the gospel by their life. And it generates the material means to be generous to others in the community.
elements.questionHint: Paul links working faithfully with ’living a decent life before outsiders’ — what does that connection suggest about how our work habits can be a form of witness?

elements.question: Who benefits from your own work? Is anyone harmed by it? (Be specific!)
elements.sampleAnswer: My work benefits my immediate colleagues (who rely on my contribution), the people or organisations we serve, my family (through income), and the wider community (through taxes and economic participation). It may also harm: if my industry has negative environmental or social consequences I haven’t fully examined, or if my working patterns harm my family through absence, or if I cut corners in ways that disadvantage clients or colleagues. Paul’s call to ’attend to your own business’ includes this kind of honest accounting.
elements.questionHint: Paul frames work in the context of love for others — who are the actual people, near and far, who are affected by what you do each day at work?

elements.question: How could your work be a clearer expression of love for others?
elements.sampleAnswer: Diligence itself is love — when I do my work well, real people benefit. I could be more intentional about this by thinking about the end user of what I produce: who is helped when I do this well, and who is let down when I don’t? I could also look for ways to go beyond the minimum — noticing when a colleague is struggling, being generous with my time or expertise, or advocating for fairer conditions for those whose work is less visible or valued.
elements.questionHint: If Paul says that working well is connected to loving the community around you, what is one specific way your work could be done with more care or generosity toward the people it affects?

elements.text: A Heart Check

elements.instruction: Rate yourself on each statement (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).
statements: My work feels meaningless most of the time.
I envy people who have more ’spiritual’ jobs.
I believe God is glorified in my daily work.
I struggle to connect Sunday worship to Monday work.
I consciously offer my work to God in prayer.

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: How might Jesus want to reshape your attitude toward your current work situation?
What would it mean for you to ’work as worship’ this week?
How could you encourage others in your church to see their daily work as part of God’s mission?

elements.text: Closing Prayer

Lord Jesus, thank you for the work you have given me to do. Forgive me when I separate my work from my worship. Teach me to see every task as service to you. Help me to love others through my labour, to endure its frustrations with hope, and to find joy in working for your glory. Amen.

Sarah is a teacher. She loves helping her students grow and develop, but she’s exhausted by the never-ending demands of school life.

Ben works in IT. His job seems far removed from ministry. He’s good at it, but he wonders if it’s really serving anyone.

Judith stays home with her two children. She often feels invisible, like her daily work doesn’t matter much to anyone.

Each of these three people wants to serve God, but none of them is sure how their work connects with loving others.

elements.question: Think about the work you do (paid or unpaid). Who benefits from it?
Do you ever struggle to see your work as an act of love?
What work does our society tend to value most, and why?

elements.text: Love is not just a feeling; it is a commitment to the good of others. Our work, whatever form it takes, is one of the main ways God calls us to love. Whether teaching, cleaning, building, parenting, or governing, work is love made visible.

elements.passageText: <sup>36</sup>“Teacher, which commandment in the law is the greatest?” <sup>37</sup>Jesus said to him, “‘Love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind.’ <sup>38</sup>This is the first and greatest commandment. <sup>39</sup>The second is like it: ‘Love your neighbor as yourself.’“

elements.question: Why is it important to see love as more than just emotion?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus commands love — and you cannot command a feeling. Love in this passage is something we are to do with all our heart, soul, and mind: it is total, active, and directed. If love were merely an emotion, we could not be held responsible for failing to love. But because love is a commitment to the good of another — expressed in action — it can be practised, cultivated, and extended even toward people we find difficult. Work is one of the primary arenas where this kind of active love is lived out.
elements.questionHint: If Jesus commands us to love, and feelings cannot be commanded, what does that tell you about the kind of love he has in mind?

elements.question: How might your everyday tasks be ways of loving others?
elements.sampleAnswer: The second great commandment is to love our neighbour as ourselves — and our neighbours include everyone our work touches. A nurse loves her patients by attentive care; an engineer loves future users of a bridge by building it safely; a parent loves their children by the routine of feeding, listening, and showing up. When I approach my tasks as acts of love toward the people they affect, the motive and quality of my work change — even the mundane becomes an expression of the great commandment.
elements.questionHint: Think about who your ’neighbour’ is in the context of your daily work — how might seeing them through the lens of the second commandment change how you approach what you do?

elements.question: What difference might it make if you began each day asking, ‘How can I love someone through my work today?’
elements.sampleAnswer: It would shift the centre of gravity from ’what do I need to get done?’ to ’who do I have the opportunity to serve?’ Tasks would remain the same, but their meaning would change. A meeting becomes an opportunity to listen well; a report becomes an act of care for the people who will rely on it; a difficult conversation becomes a chance to love someone with honesty. Starting with that question also keeps work from becoming purely self-serving, and anchors it to the great commandments Jesus gave.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says the second commandment is ’like’ the first — how might beginning the day with love for God shape the way you then approach loving the people your work touches?

elements.text: God made us relational. We are created in the image of a triune God — Father, Son, and Spirit — who works in love and partnership. Our work is not meant to be solitary achievement, but shared service.

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>Then God said, “Let us make humankind in our image, after our likeness, so they may rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air, over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over all the creatures that move on the earth.“

elements.question: How does the ’image of God’ change how we think of teamwork?
elements.sampleAnswer: God himself works in community — ‘Let us make’ — and creates humanity to reflect that communal image. We are made for collaboration, not isolated achievement. When we work well together — pooling different gifts, depending on one another, sharing credit, carrying one another’s burdens — we reflect the nature of the triune God. Teamwork is therefore not just pragmatically useful; it is theologically fitting. A team that works in genuine mutual service images God in a way a lone worker cannot.
elements.questionHint: God says ’let us make’ — in whose image are we made, and what does the plural suggest about what kind of working relationships best reflect that image?

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Two people are better than one, because they can reap more benefit from their labor. <sup>10</sup>For if they fall, one will help his companion up – but pity the person who falls down and has no one to help him up! <sup>11</sup>Furthermore, if two lie together, they can keep each other warm, but how can one person keep warm by himself? <sup>12</sup>Although an assailant may overpower one person alone, two can withstand him. Moreover, a three-stranded cord is not quickly broken.

elements.question: Why is working together wise?
elements.sampleAnswer: The Teacher gives three concrete reasons: productivity (two reap more benefit from their combined labour), resilience (one can help the other up when they fall), and strength (two can withstand what would overwhelm one). Working together builds in protection against failure, isolation, and attack. The wisdom here is deeply practical — collaboration is not just emotionally satisfying; it is structurally sounder. The three-stranded cord image adds a further dimension: strength increases not just additively but exponentially when people are genuinely bound together.
elements.questionHint: The Teacher gives several different reasons why two are better than one — which of his examples speaks most directly to a challenge you face in your own work, and why?

elements.question: How does working with others help us grow in love and humility?
elements.sampleAnswer: Working with others forces us to depend on people who are different from us, to receive help when we fall, and to offer help when it costs us something. These are exactly the conditions in which pride is challenged and love is practised. The image of lifting a fallen companion is a picture of costly, unglamorous love — not the kind that feels inspiring, but the kind that shows up when someone needs help getting back on their feet. Collaboration consistently confronts our preference for self-sufficiency.
elements.questionHint: Verse 10 describes one companion helping another who has fallen — what does that kind of help require from the person giving it, and how might regularly needing and offering that kind of help form our character?

elements.text: Work allows us to love many people in a few ways (through our tasks and skills) while relationships allow us to love a few people in many ways (through personal care and time). A doctor loves many people in a few ways (through medical skill). A parent loves a few people in many ways (through daily acts of care).

elements.question: Who are the ’many’ people your work serves? (Try to list them.)
Who are the ’few’ people God has placed in your care personally?
How often do you over-emphasise one kind of love (task or relationship) and neglect the other?

elements.text: Work (like all love) is often hard and imperfect. We will sometimes do work that feels fruitless or frustrating. Yet even difficult, unseen labour can be love if it seeks the good of others and the glory of God.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Love must be without hypocrisy. Abhor what is evil, cling to what is good. <sup>10</sup>Be devoted to one another with mutual love, showing eagerness in honoring one another. <sup>11</sup>Do not lag in zeal, be enthusiastic in spirit, serve the Lord. <sup>12</sup>Rejoice in hope, endure in suffering, persist in prayer. <sup>13</sup>Contribute to the needs of the saints, pursue hospitality.

elements.question: How does Paul connect love, zeal, and service?
elements.sampleAnswer: For Paul, genuine love is characterised by zeal and devotion — it is not passive or half-hearted. He explicitly warns against lagging in zeal, because love that grows lazy stops being love in any meaningful sense. Service to the Lord and love for others are presented as inseparable: the source of zeal is not personal ambition but spiritual fervour, grounded in hope and sustained by prayer. Love without enthusiasm becomes mere duty; zeal without love becomes performance. Paul holds them together.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’do not lag in zeal’ right alongside commands to love and serve — what does the presence of that warning suggest about the relationship between love and effort?

elements.question: What does it mean to ’serve the Lord’ in ordinary work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s ’serve the Lord’ sits in the middle of a list of very practical, relational instructions — honouring others, contributing to needs, pursuing hospitality. Serving the Lord is therefore not abstracted from daily life but expressed through it. In ordinary work, serving the Lord means bringing the same enthusiasm and integrity I would bring if Jesus were physically present watching. It means treating colleagues and clients as people made in God’s image, doing unseen work with the same care as visible work, and refusing to let expediency replace honesty.
elements.questionHint: Paul places ’serve the Lord’ alongside instructions like pursuing hospitality and meeting needs — what does that context suggest about where serving the Lord actually happens?

elements.question: How might loving others through work require sacrifice?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s list includes enduring suffering and persisting in prayer — neither of which is comfortable. Loving through work can mean staying with a difficult task when we would rather give up, continuing to serve someone who does not appreciate it, contributing to others’ needs even when our own resources feel stretched, or absorbing the cost of doing the right thing when cutting corners would be easier. The hospitality Paul mentions was genuinely costly in the ancient world. Love made visible through work often involves giving something of ourselves.
elements.questionHint: Paul mentions enduring in suffering as part of the same passage about love and service — what does it tell us that sacrifice is included in his description of what love in practice looks like?

elements.text: True greatness is found in serving. When we work for the good of others, we follow the pattern of our Saviour, who gave himself in love. Our work becomes a small echo of his great act of redemption.

elements.passageText: <sup>42</sup>Jesus called them and said to them, “You know that those who are recognized as rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and those in high positions act as tyrants over them. <sup>43</sup>But it is not this way among you. Instead whoever wants to be great among you must be your servant, <sup>44</sup>and whoever wants to be first among you must be the slave of all. <sup>45</sup>For even the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.“

elements.question: How does Jesus redefine greatness?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus turns the world’s definition of greatness completely upside down. In the surrounding culture — and in most workplaces today — greatness means being served, having authority over others, and accumulating influence. Jesus says the greatest is the servant of all, and he points to himself as the model: the Son of Man, who had every right to be served, came to serve and to give his life. Greatness in Jesus’ kingdom is measured by how much you give, not how much you receive.
elements.questionHint: Jesus contrasts two models of leadership — those who ’lord it over’ others and those who serve — which model does the world around you tend to reward, and what does Jesus say about that?

elements.question: What would it look like to serve like Jesus in your workplace?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus’ service was costly, concrete, and directed at real people’s needs. In the workplace, serving like Jesus might mean: taking on tasks below my status without resentment; using my position or skills to open doors for others rather than to advance myself; protecting a vulnerable colleague when it would be easier to stay silent; or doing the unglamorous work that no one else wants to do — and doing it well, without needing credit. It also means resisting the pull toward using authority to control rather than to serve.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says whoever wants to be first must be ’slave of all’ — what would it look like in your specific workplace context to put the interests of those around you before your own advancement?

elements.question: How might ambition or pride compete with love in your work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Ambition becomes problematic when it shifts my focus from what I can give to what I can gain — when I start managing my reputation rather than doing good work, or when I protect my position rather than serve those in my care. Pride shows up when I resent having to help someone who earns less, or when I feel diminished by doing mundane tasks. Jesus’ disciples were arguing about who would be greatest — that same impulse is alive in most workplaces. The antidote is regularly returning to the image of Jesus, who had all authority and used it to wash feet.
elements.questionHint: The disciples were arguing about positions of greatness right before Jesus spoke these words — in what ways might the desire for status or recognition quietly shape your own motivations at work?

elements.text: A Heart Check

elements.instruction: Rate yourself on each statement (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).
statements: I see my work primarily as a way to earn money, not to love others.
I consciously think about who my work benefits.
I struggle to love the people I work with.
I feel my job is too small to make a real difference.
I want to serve others more intentionally through my work.

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Who has loved you through their work? What impact did that have on your life or faith?
What practices could help you approach your work as service?
How might your church community affirm different kinds of work as genuine acts of love?

elements.text: Closing Prayer

Father, thank you for making work part of your good creation. Thank you that in Jesus I am free to work, not for approval, but out of love. Help me to see my daily tasks as opportunities to serve my neighbours. Teach me to love many people through my work, and a few people deeply in my relationships. May my work reflect your goodness and grace. Amen.

Daniel works for a manufacturing company that makes packaging for consumer goods. He’s good at his job and manages a small team. But he’s been feeling restless:
“I know my job pays the bills, but sometimes I wonder if what I do really matters to God. I’m not preaching the gospel. I’m not changing the world. I’m just … making boxes.”
Daniel isn’t lazy; he’s longing for meaning. He wants to believe that his work counts for more than money.

elements.question: Have you questioned if your work has lasting value?
What kinds of work are most likely to be described as ’meaningful’ or ’important’ in your culture?
Why do you think Christians sometimes separate ’spiritual’ work from ’ordinary’ work?

elements.text: Work begins before sin, as part of creation’s goodness. When God made humanity in his image, he gave us work — not as punishment, but as privilege. We share in God’s creative and sustaining activity.

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>Then God said, “Let us make humankind in our image, after our likeness, so they may rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air, over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over all the creatures that move on the earth.” <sup>27</sup>God created humankind in his own image, in the image of God he created them, male and female he created them. <sup>28</sup>God blessed them and said to them, “Be fruitful and multiply! Fill the earth and subdue it! Rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air and every creature that moves on the ground.“
31God saw all that he had made – and it was very good! There was evening, and there was morning, the sixth day.

elements.question: What do we learn about God’s view of work?
elements.sampleAnswer: God gave humanity the mandate to fill, subdue, and rule the earth — and then declared the whole of creation, including humanity and their God-given role, ‘very good’. Work is therefore embedded in God’s original and positive assessment of creation. It was not an afterthought or a necessary evil, but part of what God declared excellent. We also learn that humanity’s work is an extension of God’s own creative activity: we are image-bearers, which means our ruling and caring for the world is a reflection of God’s own sovereign care.
elements.questionHint: God gives humanity a mandate to rule and subdue in verse 28, and then in verse 31 calls everything ’very good’ — what does that sequence tell you about how God evaluates the work he has given us to do?

elements.question: What difference might it make if we viewed all honest work as part of God’s good design?
elements.sampleAnswer: It would remove the artificial hierarchy between ’spiritual’ and ’secular’ work. A factory worker participating in the mandate to steward and develop the earth has as much dignity before God as a pastor. It would also change how we handle drudgery and frustration: rather than seeing mundane work as meaningless, we would see it as part of a larger, God-endorsed project of human flourishing. And it would make Sunday’s worship and Monday’s work feel continuous rather than compartmentalised.
elements.questionHint: If God declared the work-mandate he gave humanity to be ’very good’, how might that change the way you view work that society tends to consider low-status or unimportant?

elements.text: Adam’s work wasn’t to create something from nothing. He was reshaping what God had already made. All good work is like that: we take the raw materials of creation and shape them for good purposes.

elements.question: What ’raw materials’ do you work with? (Physical, intellectual, relational, or creative?)
How does your work join in God’s ongoing care for his world?
How does your work show creativity, order, or stewardship?

elements.text: After the Fall, work became painful, frustrating, and sometimes fruitless. We still work, but now we face the ’thorns and thistles’ of broken systems, strained relationships, and our own weakness. Yet work remains good. Its goodness is not erased by sin, only distorted by it.

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>But to Adam he said, “Because you obeyed your wife and ate from the tree about which I commanded you, ‘You must not eat from it,‘ cursed is the ground thanks to you; in painful toil you will eat of it all the days of your life. <sup>18</sup>It will produce thorns and thistles for you, but you will eat the grain of the field. <sup>19</sup>By the sweat of your brow you will eat food until you return to the ground, for out of it you were taken; for you are dust, and to dust you will return.“

elements.question: What can we expect fallen work to be like?
elements.sampleAnswer: Fallen work is marked by resistance, toil, and mortality. The ground — representing the created order we work within — is now cursed, meaning it will fight back against our efforts rather than readily yielding to them. Thorns and thistles are the unintended consequences, the problems that arise unbidden, the inefficiencies and conflicts that make work hard. And work is now shadowed by death: we labour knowing that we will not last, and that what we build may not outlast us either. Fallen work is still work, but it is work against the grain.
elements.questionHint: God says the ground is cursed ’because of you’ — what does that tell you about the relationship between human sin and the difficulty we experience in work?

elements.question: Why is it important to affirm that work is still good, even when it feels broken?
elements.sampleAnswer: If we conclude that work is bad because it is hard, we may either abandon it (seeking escape through leisure or ’purely spiritual’ activity) or become cynical and joyless in it. But the curse falls on the ground, not on the mandate to work. Work itself remains God’s good gift; what has changed is the conditions under which we do it. Affirming this protects us from both work-despair (giving up on meaningful labour) and work-idolatry (thinking a perfect job would solve everything). The brokenness is real, but so is the goodness.
elements.questionHint: Notice that God curses the ground but does not withdraw the mandate to work — what does that distinction suggest about whether work itself is the problem?

elements.question: How might remembering the Fall help you respond with grace and patience to workplace frustration?
elements.sampleAnswer: When I understand that thorns and thistles are the expected condition of fallen work — not an aberration or a personal failing — I can meet frustration with realism rather than despair or anger. A difficult colleague, a project that collapses, a system that grinds against good intentions: these are not surprises in a fallen world. They are what Genesis 3 predicts. That doesn’t mean accepting injustice, but it does mean I can endure ordinary frustration with patience, knowing it is part of a bigger story that does not end in Genesis 3.
elements.questionHint: If Genesis 3 tells us that resistance and frustration are what work in a fallen world will feel like, how might expecting them — rather than being surprised by them — change how you respond when they arrive?

elements.text:
Some work reflects God’s creation design: cultivating what he made (farming, teaching, building). Some work responds to the Fall: restraining evil, healing, repairing (medicine, law, policing). Some work points to redemption: proclaiming the gospel, showing mercy, building community. All these glorify God when done faithfully.

God’s plan is not to scrap creation but to renew it. So our work is not wasted — not when done in faith, hope, and love. It anticipates the coming new creation, where everything will be restored under Christ’s lordship. Every faithful act of work, every moment of truth, justice, beauty, and care, will find its fulfilment in his kingdom.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>For I consider that our present sufferings cannot even be compared to the glory that will be revealed to us. <sup>19</sup>For the creation eagerly waits for the revelation of the sons of God. <sup>20</sup>For the creation was subjected to futility – not willingly but because of God who subjected it – in hope <sup>21</sup>that the creation itself will also be set free from the bondage of decay into the glorious freedom of God’s children. <sup>22</sup>For we know that the whole creation groans and suffers together until now. <sup>23</sup>Not only this, but we ourselves also, who have the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly as we eagerly await our adoption, the redemption of our bodies. <sup>24</sup>For in hope we were saved. Now hope that is seen is not hope, because who hopes for what he sees? <sup>25</sup>But if we hope for what we do not see, we eagerly wait for it with endurance.

1Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and earth had ceased to exist, and the sea existed no more. 2And I saw the holy city – the new Jerusalem – descending out of heaven from God, made ready like a bride adorned for her husband. 3And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying: “Look! The residence of God is among human beings. He will live among them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them. 4He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death will not exist any more – or mourning, or crying, or pain, for the former things have ceased to exist.” 5And the one seated on the throne said: “Look! I am making all things new!” Then he said to me, “Write it down, because these words are reliable and true.”

elements.question: What hope do we have for the future of creation, and our work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Romans 8 tells us that creation’s groaning is not its final state — it was subjected to futility in hope, and it eagerly awaits liberation. God’s plan is renewal, not abandonment. Revelation 21 confirms this: God makes all things new, and comes to dwell with his people in a renewed creation. This means our work in the present, done faithfully and in hope, is not ultimately wasted. What we build in the direction of truth, justice, beauty, and care participates in a story that ends in new creation — our labour is not lost, only not yet complete.
elements.questionHint: Paul says creation was subjected to futility ’in hope’ — and Revelation 21 says God will make all things new rather than all new things: what does that distinction suggest about the relationship between our present work and the coming kingdom?

elements.text: Worship is not limited to church gatherings. When we work with integrity, diligence, and gratitude, we are offering ourselves to God in worship. Every spreadsheet, broom, rice field, lesson, or repair can be part of a life that says, ‘Thank you, Lord.’

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Therefore I exhort you, brothers and sisters, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a sacrifice – alive, holy, and pleasing to God – which is your reasonable service.

elements.question: What might it mean to ’offer your work’ as a sacrifice of praise?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says to present our bodies — which includes everything our bodies do, including work — as a living sacrifice. Unlike a dead sacrifice, a living one keeps moving, keeps engaging with the world. Offering work as praise means doing it with conscious attention to God: acknowledging that the ability to work comes from him, that the people I serve are his image-bearers, and that the quality of my effort reflects on his name. It means working as an act of gratitude — ‘I do this because of your mercies to me, Lord.’
elements.questionHint: Paul says this offering of ourselves is our ’reasonable service’ in response to God’s mercies — what does that suggest about the motivation that should drive our work?

elements.question: How can you practically dedicate your work to God each day?
elements.sampleAnswer: A short prayer at the start of the working day — offering the day’s tasks to God, naming the people I will serve, and asking for the Spirit’s help — turns the beginning of work into an act of worship. I could also build in moments of recollection during the day: before a meeting, before a difficult task, before an interaction I am dreading. The point is to keep conscious the reality that my working body is a living sacrifice — not as a burden, but as a response of gratitude to the God who has been merciful to me.
elements.questionHint: Paul’s language of ’presenting’ your body suggests a deliberate, conscious act — what might it look like to make that offering intentionally at the start of each working day rather than accidentally or not at all?

elements.text: A Heart Check

elements.instruction: Rate yourself on each statement (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).
statements: I often see my work as a necessary evil rather than something good.
I believe that all honest work can glorify God.
I can identify how my work serves others and contributes to God’s world.
I struggle to believe my work has value in God’s eyes.
I want my work to reflect the hope of the New Creation.

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: How can your work participate in God’s purposes?
What practices help you connect your faith and your work?
How could you encourage others to see the goodness of their work, especially those who feel overlooked or undervalued?

elements.text: Closing Prayer

Lord God, thank you for the gift of work. Thank you that in Jesus even our ordinary tasks can bring you glory. Forgive us when we despise the work you’ve given or seek meaning apart from you. Help us to work faithfully, creatively, and with love: to bring order from chaos, healing from hurt, and beauty from brokenness. May all our labour reflect your goodness and anticipate the day when you make all things new. Amen.

Lydia started her own interior design business ten years ago. She began small, working from her home, but her company has grown rapidly. She now employs twelve people, has steady contracts, and earns more than she ever imagined. She thanks God for the blessings, but lately she feels uneasy.
“I’ve worked hard, and I try to be generous. But it’s easy to feel anxious about the future, and to always want just a bit more.”
Lydia is beginning to wonder: Is her success drawing her closer to God, or away from him?

elements.question: What kind of ’fruit’ does your work produce: financial, relational, social, spiritual?
How do you tend to measure success at work?
When does ambition become dangerous for the heart?

elements.text: Work is a partnership with God’s provision. Our effort matters, but all fruit ultimately comes from his hand. We honour God by working faithfully and wisely, and remembering that every result is grace.

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>Be careful not to say, “My own ability and skill have produced all this wealth for me.” <sup>18</sup>You must remember the Lord your God, for he is the one who gives ability to get wealth; if you do this he will confirm his covenant that he swore to your ancestors, even as he has to this day.

elements.question: What attitude should we have towards work and success?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses warns Israel against the most natural human response to prosperity: self-congratulation. The attitude God requires is one of remembrance — actively recalling that every ability, skill, and opportunity that produced the wealth came ultimately from God. This is not passivity (we still work, develop skills, make decisions), but it is humility: holding success with an open hand, recognising that we are stewards of gifts rather than self-made people. Gratitude rather than pride is the right posture before God in success.
elements.questionHint: Moses says to ’be careful’ not to make a particular claim about your success — what is the specific thought he warns against, and why might it be so easy to think that way after working hard?

elements.question: How does gratitude protect us from pride?
elements.sampleAnswer: Pride says ’I did this’; gratitude says ’this was given to me’. When I remember that my abilities, my opportunities, my health, my education, and the conditions that made my success possible were all gifts from God, pride has less ground to stand on. Gratitude keeps me aware of my dependence — and dependence is incompatible with arrogance. Moses frames this as an act of memory: ‘you must remember’. Gratitude is a discipline of remembering who gave the ability, which continually resets our posture before God.
elements.questionHint: Moses connects remembering God with resisting pride — how does keeping in mind where your abilities ultimately come from change the story you tell yourself about your own success?

elements.text: Wealth is stored work — a way of storing the fruit of our labour — and it is meant to serve love. God entrusts resources to us so that we might meet needs, build community, and share joy. The goal of work’s fruit is relationship, not self-protection.

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>Command those who are rich in this world’s goods not to be haughty or to set their hope on riches, which are uncertain, but on God who richly provides us with all things for our enjoyment. <sup>18</sup>Tell them to do good, to be rich in good deeds, to be generous givers, sharing with others. <sup>19</sup>In this way they will save up a treasure for themselves as a firm foundation for the future and so lay hold of what is truly life.

elements.question: What is the danger associated with having wealth?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul identifies two intertwined dangers: haughtiness (the pride that comes from having more than others) and misplaced hope (treating wealth as a foundation for security rather than trusting in God). Wealth can quietly become the thing we rely on for safety, identity, and significance — replacing God in practice even while we acknowledge him in theory. The passage also implies that the uncertainty of riches is itself the exposure of this danger: what we set our hope on must be able to bear the weight of that hope, and wealth cannot.
elements.questionHint: Paul says riches are ’uncertain’ — why might the very uncertainty of wealth make it a particularly dangerous thing to place our hope in?

elements.question: What might being ’rich in good deeds’ look like for you?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s counterpoint to being rich in money is being rich in good deeds — an abundance of purposeful, others-directed action. For me, this might mean: using my professional skills pro bono for people who could not otherwise afford them; being quick to meet a practical need rather than waiting to be asked; investing time in mentoring someone less experienced; or giving financially in a way that requires something of me rather than only from surplus. The standard is richness — not a minimal tick-box, but overflowing generosity.
elements.questionHint: Paul places ’being rich in good deeds’ as the alternative to being rich in wealth — what would it mean for your generosity to be as abundant as the word ’rich’ implies, rather than just adequate?

elements.text:
Work bears many kinds of fruit:
  • Provision — meeting our needs and others’
  • Service — improving lives through skill and creativity
  • Formation — shaping our character through discipline
  • Witness — showing integrity and compassion that point to Christ
Fruitful work isn’t measured only by output, but by goodness.

elements.question: Which type of fruit do you most notice in your work?
Which do you most easily overlook?

elements.text: Work that produces wealth without worship is empty. Greed shrinks the heart, turning God’s gifts into idols.

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>Then he said to them, “Watch out and guard yourself from all types of greed, because one’s life does not consist in the abundance of his possessions.” <sup>16</sup>He then told them a parable: “The land of a certain rich man produced an abundant crop, <sup>17</sup>so he thought to himself, ‘What should I do, for I have nowhere to store my crops?’ <sup>18</sup>Then he said, ‘I will do this: I will tear down my barns and build bigger ones, and there I will store all my grain and my goods. <sup>19</sup>And I will say to myself, “You have plenty of goods stored up for many years; relax, eat, drink, celebrate!“‘ <sup>20</sup>But God said to him, ‘You fool! This very night your life will be demanded back from you, but who will get what you have prepared for yourself?’ <sup>21</sup>So it is with the one who stores up riches for himself, but is not rich toward God.“

elements.question: How does Jesus redefine what ’life’ really consists of?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus says explicitly that life does not consist in the abundance of possessions — a direct challenge to the assumption that more security, more comfort, and more wealth equals more life. The rich man had genuinely abundant crops, yet Jesus calls him a fool because he had arranged his whole future around his goods rather than around God. True life, Jesus implies, is found in being ’rich toward God’ — in a relationship of trust, dependence, and generosity rather than self-sufficiency and accumulation.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says life does not consist in possessions, and then contrasts the rich man’s approach with being ’rich toward God’ — what do you think it means to be rich toward God rather than rich in goods?

elements.question: What warning do you most need to hear in this parable?
elements.sampleAnswer: The warning I most need is the one about the internal monologue of the rich man: he speaks entirely to himself (‘I will do this… I will store… I will say to myself’). His planning involved no one else — no neighbours, no God, only himself and his goods. That kind of inward-turning is easy to slide into when work is going well. The parable warns me against the illusion that a full barn equals a secure future, and against the habit of planning for life while neglecting the God who gives and can take it.
elements.questionHint: Notice how many times the rich man says ’I’ and ’my’ in his inner monologue — what is missing from his thinking that God’s verdict at the end of the parable exposes?

elements.text: Fruitful work isn’t only about personal wealth; it’s also about justice. Christians are called to use their resources, influence, and skills to promote fairness and mercy. Generosity is not optional; it’s the natural overflow of knowing God’s grace.

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>With what should I enter the Lord’s presence? With what should I bow before the sovereign God? Should I enter his presence with burnt offerings, with year-old calves? <sup>7</sup>Will the Lord accept a thousand rams, or ten thousand streams of olive oil? Should I give him my firstborn child as payment for my rebellion, my offspring – my own flesh and blood – for my sin? <sup>8</sup>He has told you, O man, what is good, and what the Lord really wants from you: He wants you to promote justice, to be faithful, and to live obediently before your God.

elements.question: What does God require of us?
elements.sampleAnswer: Micah strips away the assumption that God is primarily impressed by religious performance or costly offerings, and instead names three intertwined requirements: promoting justice (acting to put things right in the world), being faithful (showing loyal love — hesed — to others), and walking humbly with God (living in dependent relationship with him rather than autonomous self-sufficiency). These are not additions to faith but expressions of it. God requires not more religiosity but more justice, more faithful love, and more genuine humility.
elements.questionHint: Micah contrasts increasingly extravagant religious offerings with three simple requirements — what does that contrast tell you about what God actually values over impressive acts of piety?

elements.question: How might your work environment or community benefit from your generosity to some need, or advocacy towards some justice?
elements.sampleAnswer: Micah’s call to ’promote justice’ is active — it requires looking around and noticing where things are not as they should be, then using whatever influence or resources I have to move toward fairness. In my workplace, that might mean speaking up for a colleague who is being treated unfairly, using my skills to serve a community organisation, advocating for better conditions for those lower in the pay structure, or simply being honest in contexts where dishonesty is normalised. Faithful love (hesed) makes these acts relational rather than merely transactional.
elements.questionHint: Micah calls us to ’promote justice’ — where in your specific work environment or community do you see something that falls short of justice, and what resources or influence do you have that could be used to address it?

elements.text: When we give, we participate in God’s economy of grace. Generosity multiplies joy — both ours and others’. The more we release, the more space God creates for new fruit to grow.

elements.passageText: <sup>24</sup>One person is generous and yet grows more wealthy, but another withholds more than he should and comes to poverty. <sup>25</sup>A generous person will be enriched, and the one who provides water for others will himself be satisfied.

elements.question: What is God’s view of generosity?
elements.sampleAnswer: Proverbs presents generosity as counterintuitively enriching and withholding as counterintuitively impoverishing. God’s view is that the generous person does not lose but gains — not necessarily in a simple material sense, but in the broader sense of a flourishing life. The image of providing water for others and being satisfied yourself suggests that generosity operates within God’s economy of abundance rather than the world’s economy of scarcity. When we give as God designed, we participate in a flow of blessing that returns to us.
elements.questionHint: Proverbs says the generous person grows wealthier while the one who withholds comes to poverty — how does that invert our natural assumption about the relationship between giving and having?

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that although he was rich, he became poor for your sakes, so that you by his poverty could become rich.

<sup>6</sup>My point is this: The person who sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and the person who sows generously will also reap generously. <sup>7</sup>Each one of you should give just as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, because God loves a cheerful giver. <sup>8</sup>And God is able to make all grace overflow to you so that because you have enough of everything in every way at all times, you will overflow in every good work. <sup>9</sup>Just as it is written, “He has scattered widely, he has given to the poor; his righteousness remains forever.” <sup>10</sup>Now God who provides seed for the sower and bread for food will provide and multiply your supply of seed and will cause the harvest of your righteousness to grow. <sup>11</sup>You will be enriched in every way so that you may be generous on every occasion, which is producing through us thanksgiving to God,

elements.question: What truths encourage you to give generously?
elements.sampleAnswer: Several truths in these passages are genuinely motivating: first, that Jesus himself became poor so that I could be rich — my generosity is a response to his prior, far greater generosity. Second, that God provides seed for the sower — the resources I give from are not self-generated but continuously supplied by God, so giving is not ultimately a threat to my security. Third, that the goal of God’s enriching of me is so that I may be generous on every occasion — I am resourced in order to give, not resourced in order to hoard. And fourth, that cheerful giving is what God delights in — generosity is meant to be joyful, not dutiful.
elements.questionHint: Paul grounds his appeal for generosity in what Jesus did in 8:9 — how might remembering the cost of Christ’s generosity toward you change the way you think about the cost of your own generosity toward others?

elements.text: A Heart Check

elements.instruction: Rate yourself on each statement (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).
statements: I believe my success at work is mostly due to my own effort.
I see the money I earn as belonging first to God.
I regularly thank God for the fruit my work produces.
I find it difficult to be generous when finances are tight.
I want my work to bless others, not just myself.

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: What is one practical way you could use your work’s fruit (money, skill, influence) to bless others this month?
How does generosity at work or in life display the gospel?
What might ’contentment’ look like in your stage of life?

elements.text: Closing Prayer

Generous Father, you are the giver of every good and perfect gift. Thank you for the fruit that comes from our work: for income, relationships, and growth. Guard us from greed and self-reliance. Make us wise and open-handed stewards of all you provide. May our work bear good fruit that blesses others and points them to your grace in Jesus Christ. Amen.

Samuel works as an accountant in a large company. He loves numbers and enjoys his colleagues, but recently the company’s leadership made a decision he feels uneasy about — quietly shifting costs to make the books look better for investors. Samuel knows it isn’t illegal, but it doesn’t feel right. When he raised questions, his manager told him:
“This is how the business world works. Don’t overthink it.”
Now he feels torn between loyalty to his employer and obedience to his conscience.

elements.question: Have you ever faced an ethical dilemma in your workplace? How did you handle it?
What pressures make it hard to live out your faith at work?
What might it mean to live faithfully for God in the middle of a complex organisation?

elements.text: Work is never done in isolation. Even the most independent role connects with others: customers, colleagues, supervisors, or society at large. That means every workplace has a moral atmosphere — a shared pattern of values, priorities, and pressures.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Slaves, obey your human masters with fear and trembling, in the sincerity of your heart as to Christ, <sup>6</sup>not like those who do their work only when someone is watching – as people-pleasers – but as slaves of Christ doing the will of God from the heart. <sup>7</sup>Obey with enthusiasm, as though serving the Lord and not people, <sup>8</sup>because you know that each person, whether slave or free, if he does something good, this will be rewarded by the Lord. <sup>9</sup>Masters, treat your slaves the same way, giving up the use of threats, because you know that both you and they have the same master in heaven, and there is no favoritism with him.

elements.question: How should we do our work, and how should we lead others?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul calls workers to serve with sincerity and enthusiasm — not as people-pleasers who only perform when watched, but as those whose true audience is Christ. The quality and honesty of our work should be the same whether the boss is present or absent, because God always is. For those in leadership, Paul demands an equally demanding standard: no threats, no favouritism, no exploitation. Both workers and leaders are accountable to the same heavenly Master who shows no partiality — which levels the playing field and holds everyone to the same standard of character.
elements.questionHint: Paul says to work ’as to the Lord and not for people’ — how does having Christ as your true audience change the way you approach your work when no manager is watching?

elements.question: How does working ’for the Lord’ change the way we relate to earthly supervisors or employees?
elements.sampleAnswer: When Christ is my true master, my earthly supervisor becomes a secondary authority — one I respect and serve diligently, but whose approval is not my ultimate goal. This frees me from both people-pleasing (doing whatever it takes to be liked) and from resentment (feeling exploited when unappreciated). Similarly, a leader who knows they answer to a Lord who shows no favouritism cannot treat their employees as disposable or threaten them into compliance. The awareness of a higher authority transforms both the quality of our work and the ethics of our leadership.
elements.questionHint: Paul says both workers and masters have the same Master in heaven who shows no favouritism — how might that shared accountability change the dynamic between people of different ranks in a workplace?

elements.question: What might this look like in your workplace?
elements.sampleAnswer: In practice, it might mean doing thorough work on a Friday afternoon when no one is likely to check; giving honest feedback in a meeting even when flattery would go down better; resisting the temptation to cut corners when a deadline is tight; or, as a manager, setting expectations through encouragement rather than fear. It also means doing the right thing in situations like Samuel’s — where the pressure is to go along with something that feels wrong — because my ultimate review comes from someone who cannot be deceived.
elements.questionHint: Think of a specific moment in your working week when no one would notice if you took a shortcut — how might Paul’s words here change what you do in that moment?

elements.text: Workplaces mirror larger systems of power. Authority is not evil — it’s meant to serve the good of others. But when power becomes self-serving, injustice follows. Christians are called to use influence humbly and to seek fairness for all.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>A king brings stability to a land by justice, but one who exacts tribute tears it down.

elements.question: Why is the role (and example) of a leader so important?
elements.sampleAnswer: The proverb compresses a profound truth into a single contrast: a leader who governs by justice builds stability for everyone, while a leader who uses their position to extract maximum personal benefit tears the whole community down. A leader’s orientation — toward the good of others or toward their own gain — shapes the entire atmosphere of whatever system they lead. This is why leadership character matters so much: one person at the top, practising justice or corruption, has an outsized effect on everyone beneath them.
elements.questionHint: The proverb contrasts a king who brings stability through justice with one who tears things down through self-serving taxation — what does that contrast tell you about the purpose that authority is meant to serve?

elements.question: In your role, what kind of power or influence do you hold?
elements.sampleAnswer: Even without a formal leadership title, most workers hold some form of influence: over the quality of information they pass on, the way they treat those junior to them, how they represent others in conversations, or how they use their expertise. In my role, I have influence over the people I interact with daily, the decisions I contribute to, and the standards I model. The proverb invites me to examine whether I am using that influence to create stability and fairness for those around me, or whether I am inadvertently (or deliberately) extracting advantage at others’ expense.
elements.questionHint: The proverb is about a king, but most people hold some sphere of influence — who in your working life is affected by the way you use whatever authority or influence you carry?

elements.question: How can you use it to serve and not exploit others?
elements.sampleAnswer: Using influence to serve rather than exploit means consistently asking: who benefits from the decisions I make, and who bears the cost? It means being transparent about information rather than hoarding it for strategic advantage; advocating for fair treatment of those with less power; refusing to pass unfair pressure downward; and being willing to absorb some cost personally rather than shifting it onto others. The justice the proverb commends is active — it doesn’t just avoid exploitation, it works to establish stability for the community.
elements.questionHint: The king who brings stability does so through justice — what would it look like for you to use your specific influence actively to make things more fair, rather than simply not making them worse?

elements.text: Daniel served in a pagan government, yet refused to compromise his loyalty to God. His integrity was public, costly, and consistent. He worked within a broken system, but wasn’t controlled by it.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>It seemed good to Darius to appoint over the kingdom one hundred twenty satraps who would be in charge of the entire kingdom. <sup>2</sup>Over them would be three supervisors, one of whom was Daniel. These satraps were accountable to them so that the king would not be troubled. <sup>3</sup>Daniel distinguished himself above the supervisors and satraps because an extraordinary spirit was in him, and the king planned to appoint him over the entire kingdom. <sup>4</sup>Consequently the supervisors and satraps were trying to find some pretext against Daniel in regard to the kingdom. But they were unable to find any such damaging evidence, because he was trustworthy and guilty of no negligence or corruption. <sup>5</sup>So these men said, “We won’t find any pretext against this man Daniel unless it is in connection with the law of his God.” <sup>6</sup>So the supervisors and satraps came by collusion to the king and said to him, “O King Darius, live forever! <sup>7</sup>To all the supervisors of the kingdom, the prefects, satraps, counselors, and governors it seemed like a good idea for a royal edict to be issued and an interdict to be enforced: For the next thirty days anyone who prays to any god or human being except to you, O king, should be thrown into a den of lions. <sup>8</sup>Now let the king issue a written interdict so that it cannot be altered, according to the law of the Medes and Persians, which cannot be revoked.” <sup>9</sup>So King Darius issued the written interdict. <sup>10</sup>When Daniel learned that a written decree had been issued, he went to his home where the windows in his upper room opened toward Jerusalem. Three times daily he was kneeling and praying and giving thanks to his God, just as he had been doing previously.

elements.question: What can we learn from Daniel’s balance of loyalty and faithfulness?
elements.sampleAnswer: Daniel is a model of excellence within a system he did not choose. He served Darius with genuine diligence and integrity — so much so that his enemies could find no fault in his professional conduct and had to target his religious practice instead. Daniel’s loyalty to his employer was real, but it had a limit: when the king’s decree directly conflicted with his loyalty to God, he continued to pray just as he had always done. He did not make a scene or issue a protest; he simply remained faithful to his established practice. His resistance was quiet, personal, and principled.
elements.questionHint: Daniel’s enemies searched his professional life for a fault and found nothing — what does that tell you about the relationship between genuine competence at work and the credibility of our witness?

elements.question: When might obedience to God require courage or quiet resistance?
elements.sampleAnswer: Daniel’s situation shows that resistance does not always mean confrontation. His courage was expressed through continuity — continuing to pray as he always had, even knowing the cost. In a modern workplace, obedience to God might require refusing to sign off on figures that misrepresent the truth; declining to join in the treatment of a colleague that everyone else tolerates; maintaining honesty in a context where exaggeration is the norm; or simply refusing to stay silent when something wrong is being normalised. The courage required is often not dramatic, but it is real.
elements.questionHint: Daniel did not protest or announce his resistance — he simply continued what he had always done, at personal cost. What does that suggest about what faithful resistance can look like in practice?

elements.text: Your workplace is your mission field. Your honesty and grace speak loudly — not self-righteousness, but faithfulness that points to Christ.

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>“You are the salt of the earth. But if salt loses its flavor, how can it be made salty again? It is no longer good for anything except to be thrown out and trampled on by people. <sup>14</sup>You are the light of the world. A city located on a hill cannot be hidden. <sup>15</sup>People do not light a lamp and put it under a basket but on a lampstand, and it gives light to all in the house. <sup>16</sup>In the same way, let your light shine before people, so that they can see your good deeds and give honor to your Father in heaven.“

elements.question: What difference does Jesus call us to make?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus uses two images that emphasise distinctive influence: salt preserves and flavours — it changes what it touches — and light illuminates and cannot be hidden once it is lit. Jesus is calling his followers to make a genuine, noticeable difference in the world around them. Crucially, the difference is made not by loudly announcing Christian identity but by ’good deeds’ that others can see and that point them to God. The workplace is one of the primary arenas where that difference is either made or suppressed.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says light is not lit in order to be hidden under a basket — what does that suggest about whether Christian faithfulness in the workplace is meant to be invisible to those around us?

elements.question: Who might be quietly watching your example?
elements.sampleAnswer: People rarely announce that they are watching. But a colleague who has heard I am a Christian may be paying close attention to how I react when I am treated unfairly, whether I am honest when honesty is costly, how I speak about people who are not in the room, and whether my Friday afternoon work is as good as my Monday morning work. Junior colleagues, sceptical friends, people going through difficulties — all of them may be drawing conclusions about whether faith makes a real difference, based on what they observe.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says good deeds cause others to give honour to God — who in your workplace might be drawing conclusions about God based on what they see in how you work and treat people?

elements.text: Sometimes we find ourselves in organisations or industries that operate unjustly, where exploitation or corruption are normal.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Lord, who may be a guest in your sanctuary? Who may live on your holy hill? <sup>2</sup>Whoever lives with integrity and does what is right, and speaks the truth from his heart. <sup>3</sup>He does not slander with his tongue, and does no harm to his friend, and does not bring disgrace on his neighbor. <sup>4</sup>He despises a vile person, but honors those who fear the Lord. He makes firm commitments and does not renege on his promise. <sup>5</sup>He does not charge interest when he lends his money, and does not take bribes to testify against the innocent. The one who lives like this will never be shaken.

elements.question: What kind of integrity does God value?
elements.sampleAnswer: The psalm paints a detailed picture of integrity that is strikingly practical and relational. It is not just about avoiding big sins but about the texture of everyday dealings: speaking truth from the heart (not just technically accurate words); not slandering or harming reputation; keeping promises even when circumstances change and honouring them has become costly; refusing to exploit financial relationships; refusing bribes. This is integrity in its full sense — wholeness and consistency across the whole of life, not just in the areas others are watching.
elements.questionHint: The psalm includes keeping commitments ’even when it hurts’ — what does that specific detail suggest about what genuine integrity demands of us when doing the right thing becomes personally costly?

elements.question: How do you discern when to speak up, when to stay, and when to step away?
elements.sampleAnswer: The psalm gives the end-state (‘the one who lives like this will never be shaken’) but not a decision algorithm. Discernment requires weighing several things: how serious and ongoing is the wrong? Am I complicit in it or simply adjacent to it? Have I raised the concern appropriately? Is staying enabling injustice, or is my presence a restraining and redeeming influence? Are there people depending on my presence? At some point, remaining in a situation where integrity is systematically compromised may itself become a form of complicity — but that threshold is rarely clear-cut and requires wise counsel.
elements.questionHint: The psalm describes someone who refuses to take bribes and does no harm to their neighbour — at what point does remaining in a situation where such things are happening regularly make us participants rather than just witnesses?

elements.text: God doesn’t call us to survive our workplaces, but to bless them.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>“The Lord God of Israel who rules over all says to all those he sent into exile to Babylon from Jerusalem, <sup>5</sup>’Build houses and settle down. Plant gardens and eat what they produce. <sup>6</sup>Marry and have sons and daughters. Find wives for your sons and give your daughters in marriage so that they too may have sons and daughters.
