Grow in number; do not dwindle away. <sup>7</sup>Work to see that the city where I sent you as exiles enjoys peace and prosperity. Pray to the Lord for it. For as it prospers you will prosper.’“

elements.question: How was Israel to respond to those who exiled them?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s command is remarkable given the context: Babylon is the enemy that destroyed Jerusalem and carried Israel into captivity. Yet God tells his people not to resist, withdraw, or wait passively for deliverance — but to build, plant, marry, grow, and actively seek the peace and prosperity of the very city that oppresses them. They are to pray for Babylon. Their flourishing is bound up with its flourishing. This is an extraordinary call to genuine engagement with a hostile environment, not merely tolerance of it.
elements.questionHint: God tells Israel to seek the peace of Babylon — the city of their captors — not merely to endure it. What does that unexpected command suggest about the posture God calls his people to take toward the institutions and communities they find themselves in?

elements.question: What steps could you take to help your workplace flourish?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jeremiah 29 suggests that seeking the flourishing of our environment is not optional — it is a direct command from God. In the workplace, this might mean: bringing genuine care and creativity to problems rather than doing the minimum; mentoring colleagues who need support; advocating for better working conditions; being someone who raises morale rather than draining it; praying regularly for colleagues by name; or contributing to a culture of honesty, fairness, and mutual respect. The question is not just ’am I surviving this place?’ but ’is this place better because I am here?’
elements.questionHint: God promises that Israel’s prosperity is connected to Babylon’s prosperity — what does that linkage suggest about the relationship between serving our workplace well and our own flourishing within it?

elements.text: A Heart Check

elements.instruction: Rate yourself on each statement (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).
statements: I often feel powerless to make a difference in my workplace.
I try to act with integrity even when no one notices.
I find it hard to challenge unfair, unethical, or corrupt practices.
I pray regularly for my colleagues and leaders.
I believe God can use me to bring blessing where I work.

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: How can you be salt and light in your workplace?
Who could you meet with to pray for wisdom about specific challenges at work?
What would it mean for your team, department, or organisation if more people lived out the call to justice in Micah 6:8?

elements.text: Closing Prayer

Lord of all, you are the true Master of every workplace. Thank you for calling us to serve within imperfect systems and communities. Strengthen us to act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with you. Give us courage when it’s costly to do right, and grace when others fall short. May our integrity bring light, peace, and blessing to the places you have called us to work. Amen.

Naomi is a nurse who works long shifts in a busy hospital. She loves caring for people, but lately she feels drained — physically, emotionally, spiritually. She says:
“I know I’m doing something good, but I’m so tired. Every day feels like survival, not service. I can’t remember the last time I felt rested.”
Naomi still believes in God’s goodness, but she’s lost sight of hope. She wonders if the way she works (and the world works!) will ever change.

elements.question: When have you felt most weary in your work?
What does ’rest’ look like for you? And why is it so hard to find?
How do hope and rest fit together in the Christian life?

elements.text: Before sin entered the world, God built a rhythm of work and rest into creation itself. Rest is not laziness — it’s imitation of God. We rest not because we’ve finished everything, but because he reigns.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>The heavens and the earth were completed with everything that was in them. <sup>2</sup>By the seventh day God finished the work that he had been doing, and he ceased on the seventh day all the work that he had been doing. <sup>3</sup>God blessed the seventh day and made it holy because on it he ceased all the work that he had been doing in creation.

elements.question: What does God’s rest teach us about his relationship to work?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s rest is not the rest of exhaustion — an omnipotent Creator does not grow tired. It is the rest of completion and satisfaction: the work is done, it is good, and now there is a pause to enjoy and inhabit what has been made. This tells us that work has a natural rhythm — it is not meant to be endless or all-consuming. God himself modelled a pattern of purposeful work followed by deliberate rest. Rest is woven into the fabric of creation from the very beginning, before the Fall, as part of God’s good design.
elements.questionHint: God rested not because he was exhausted but because the work was complete and ’very good’ — what does that tell you about the purpose of rest, and whether it requires finishing everything first?

elements.question: How can rest be an act of trust?
elements.sampleAnswer: God blessed the seventh day and made it holy — he set it apart as significant. For us to stop work is to declare, in practice, that the world does not depend on us keeping it going. Rest is a bodily act of faith: ‘God is Lord of this; I am not indispensable.’ For those who struggle to stop — because of anxiety, perfectionism, or financial pressure — rest requires a genuine trust that God will hold together what we cannot control while we are away from our desks. It is a weekly declaration of dependence.
elements.questionHint: If the world kept going while God rested, what does that suggest about whether the world’s continuation depends on our constant effort — and what might that mean for your own resistance to stopping?

elements.question: How might ignoring rest reveal deeper spiritual problems?
elements.sampleAnswer: If rest is designed by God and modelled by him, then the compulsive inability to stop reveals something about where our trust actually lies. It may expose a functional belief that my value depends on my output, or that God cannot be trusted to provide if I am not working. It may also reveal a disordered relationship with work — using busyness to avoid stillness, relationships, or God himself. The inability to rest is often not a practical problem but a spiritual one: a symptom of anxiety, pride, or unbelief masquerading as diligence.
elements.questionHint: If God built rest into creation as a good gift, what might our resistance or inability to take it reveal about what we actually believe — about God’s sovereignty, or our own indispensability?

elements.text: After the Fall, work became toil, and rest became elusive. The Sabbath command reminded Israel that they were not slaves but God’s children. Hebrews tells us that ultimate rest is still to come in the eternal ’Sabbath’ of the new creation.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>“Remember the Sabbath day to set it apart as holy. <sup>9</sup>For six days you may labor and do all your work, <sup>10</sup>but the seventh day is a Sabbath to the Lord your God; on it you shall not do any work, you, or your son, or your daughter, or your male servant, or your female servant, or your cattle, or the resident foreigner who is in your gates. <sup>11</sup>For in six days the Lord made the heavens and the earth and the sea and all that is in them, and he rested on the seventh day; therefore the Lord blessed the Sabbath day and set it apart as holy.“

<sup>12</sup>“Be careful to observe the Sabbath day just as the Lord your God has commanded you. <sup>13</sup>You are to work and do all your tasks for six days, <sup>14</sup>but the seventh day is the Sabbath of the Lord your God. On that day you must not do any work, neither you, your son, your daughter, your male slave, your female slave, your ox, your donkey, any other animal, or the foreigner who lives with you, so that your male and female slaves may rest as well as you. <sup>15</sup>Recall that you were a slave in the land of Egypt and the Lord your God brought you out of there by strength and power. That is why the Lord your God has commanded you to observe the Sabbath day.“

elements.question: How does rest connect with our identity as God’s people?
elements.sampleAnswer: Exodus grounds the Sabbath in creation — we rest because God rested, and we are his image-bearers. Deuteronomy grounds it in redemption — Israel must rest because they were once slaves who had no rest, and God set them free. Together, these two foundations say: you rest because of who God is (Creator and Redeemer) and who you are (his image-bearers and his redeemed children — not slaves). The Sabbath is therefore an identity statement. It declares: ‘I am not defined by my productivity. I belong to a God who redeems, not a system that only values output.’
elements.questionHint: Deuteronomy roots the Sabbath command in the memory of slavery — what does it mean for freed people to rest, and how does that connect rest with freedom and identity rather than just time management?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Therefore, let us be wary that, while the promise of entering his rest remains open, none of you may seem to have come short of it. <sup>2</sup>For we had good news proclaimed to us just as they did. But the message they heard did them no good, since they did not join in with those who heard it in faith. <sup>3</sup>For we who have believed enter that rest, as he has said, “As I swore in my anger, ‘They will never enter my rest!’” And yet God’s works were accomplished from the foundation of the world. <sup>4</sup>For he has spoken somewhere about the seventh day in this way: “And God rested on the seventh day from all his works,” <sup>5</sup>but in this passage again: “They will never enter my rest!” <sup>6</sup>Therefore it remains for some to enter it, yet those to whom it was previously proclaimed did not enter because of disobedience. <sup>7</sup>So God again designates a certain day, “Today,” speaking through David after so long a time, as in the words quoted before, “Oh, that today you would listen as he speaks! Do not harden your hearts.” <sup>8</sup>For if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken afterward about another day. <sup>9</sup>Consequently a Sabbath rest remains for the people of God. <sup>10</sup>For the one who enters God’s rest has also rested from his works, just as God did from his own works. <sup>11</sup>Thus we must make every effort to enter that rest, so that no one may fall by following the same pattern of disobedience.

elements.question: Is Hebrews 4 describing physical rest, spiritual rest, or both?
elements.sampleAnswer: Hebrews 4 primarily describes a spiritual rest — the rest that comes from ceasing to work for our own salvation and trusting in God’s completed work in Christ. The writer connects God’s original seventh-day rest with the rest that believers ’enter’ through faith, and points to a final ’Sabbath rest’ still to come for God’s people. But this spiritual rest is not disconnected from physical rest — the weekly Sabbath is a sign pointing to this deeper reality. Every time we rest from physical work in trust, we rehearse the greater rest of the gospel: that God has done what we could not do for ourselves.
elements.questionHint: The writer says we ’enter’ God’s rest by faith, and connects this with ceasing from our own works just as God did from his — what might it mean to ’rest’ from trying to earn something that has already been given?

elements.question: How does this future rest shape our work today?
elements.sampleAnswer: Knowing that a final, complete rest awaits means that our present toil is not the whole story. We can work hard without working desperately, because the outcome of history is not dependent on our effort. We can stop without guilt, because the completion of all things lies in God’s hands. The promised rest also means we do not need to extract ultimate meaning or satisfaction from work in the present — that kind of fulfilment is still to come. Work today is real and valuable, but it is penultimate; the ultimate rest is ahead.
elements.questionHint: Hebrews says a Sabbath rest ’remains’ for the people of God — how might knowing that the final chapter of your story ends in rest, rather than toil, change the pressure you place on your work today to deliver everything you need?

elements.text: Work often feels frustrating because the world is still under the curse. But God’s promise is not to abandon creation — he will renew it. That means our work, done in faith, is not wasted. Every act of love, justice, and creativity anticipates the world to come.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>For I consider that our present sufferings cannot even be compared to the glory that will be revealed to us. <sup>19</sup>For the creation eagerly waits for the revelation of the sons of God. <sup>20</sup>For the creation was subjected to futility – not willingly but because of God who subjected it – in hope <sup>21</sup>that the creation itself will also be set free from the bondage of decay into the glorious freedom of God’s children. <sup>22</sup>For we know that the whole creation groans and suffers together until now. <sup>23</sup>Not only this, but we ourselves also, who have the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly as we eagerly await our adoption, the redemption of our bodies. <sup>24</sup>For in hope we were saved. Now hope that is seen is not hope, because who hopes for what he sees? <sup>25</sup>But if we hope for what we do not see, we eagerly wait for it with endurance.

elements.question: What future is there for creation (including work)?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul paints a picture of creation not as something to be escaped but as something to be liberated. The whole created order — including the world of work and human making — is groaning under futility, but it does so ’in hope’. The future is liberation: creation will be set free from the bondage of decay into the glorious freedom of God’s children. This is not annihilation but renewal. That means work done now in the direction of truth, justice, beauty, and love anticipates and participates in what God will ultimately restore.
elements.questionHint: Paul says creation was subjected to futility ’in hope’ — what does the word ’hope’ in that phrase tell you about whether creation’s present groaning is its final state?

elements.question: How can hope keep us from despair or cynicism in hard jobs?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says we wait ’with endurance’ — hope does not remove the difficulty of the present, but it reframes it. When a nurse like Naomi knows that the suffering she witnesses is not the final word — that the creation groaning around her will one day be freed — she has a reason to keep showing up that goes beyond what she can see or feel today. Hope gives work a future it would not otherwise have. It means that the care given to a patient who did not recover, or the justice sought in a system that resisted it, is not simply lost — it belongs to a story that ends in liberation.
elements.questionHint: Paul describes hope as ’waiting with endurance’ for what we cannot yet see — how does having a confident destination change what you are able to endure on the journey toward it?

elements.text: Jesus invites the weary worker to himself. He doesn’t promise less work, but a new way of working — with him, under his leadership. Rest is not found in escaping work, but in abiding in Christ.

elements.passageText: <sup>28</sup>“Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. <sup>29</sup>Take my yoke on you and learn from me, because I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. <sup>30</sup>For my yoke is easy to bear, and my load is not hard to carry.“

elements.question: How does Jesus’ promise encourage you personally?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus does not say ’stop working’ — he says ’take my yoke’. A yoke is a working implement: it enables two to pull together. His promise is not escape from labour but a different kind of labour — one that is shared with him, guided by his gentleness and humility, and not driven by the crushing burden of earning acceptance or carrying impossible expectations. The rest Jesus offers is for the soul, not only the body. It is the rest of knowing I am loved, carried, and guided by someone whose load is genuinely light compared to the weight of striving alone.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says his yoke is ’easy’ and his load ’not hard’ — but he still speaks of a yoke and a load. What does that tell you about the kind of rest he is offering, and what it is not?

elements.question: What burdens are you carrying in your work right now?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus specifically addresses the ’weary and burdened’ — which suggests naming what the burden actually is matters. Current burdens might include: the pressure to perform beyond capacity; guilt over work undone; fear of failure or redundancy; the weight of difficult relationships; carrying others’ problems without support; the exhaustion of working in an under-resourced environment; or the quiet despair of work that feels fruitless. Jesus’ invitation is to bring those specific burdens to him — not to manage them better, but to let him share the load.
elements.questionHint: Jesus invites specifically those who are ’weary and burdened’ — what would it mean to name honestly, and bring to him, the specific things that are weighing most heavily on you in your work right now?

elements.text: Resurrection guarantees that faithful labour won’t be lost. Even small acts of service matter eternally. Jesus rose, so our work matters forever.

elements.passageText: <sup>58</sup>So then, dear brothers and sisters, be firm. Do not be moved! Always be outstanding in the work of the Lord, knowing that your labor is not in vain in the Lord.

elements.question: How does the promise of resurrection encourage you personally?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul places this exhortation at the end of his great chapter on resurrection — the logic is direct: because Christ rose, our labour is not in vain. The resurrection means that nothing done in faith and love for the Lord is ultimately lost, even when it appears fruitless in the present. For Naomi, exhausted from her nursing shifts with little visible fruit, the resurrection says: the care you gave that patient who didn’t recover was not wasted — it belongs to a story that ends in life, not death. That is genuinely encouraging in a way that no earthly metric of success can be.
elements.questionHint: Paul says our labour is ’not in vain in the Lord’ — he grounds this in the resurrection earlier in the chapter. What is the connection between Christ rising from the dead and the work we do not being ultimately wasted?

elements.question: How does knowing your work has eternal value change your motivation?
elements.sampleAnswer: If my labour is not in vain in the Lord, then the motivation to work does not depend on visible results, recognition, or immediate fruit. I can work with steady, unhurried faithfulness rather than anxious striving for outcomes I cannot control. It also means I can invest in work that the world considers small or unglamorous — because eternal value is not measured by human metrics. The resurrection shifts the question from ’is this getting me anywhere?’ to ’am I being faithful?’ — and that is a much more sustainable basis for a working life.
elements.questionHint: Paul says to be ’firm’ and ’not moved’ immediately before saying labour is not in vain — what might make us tempted to give up or become half-hearted in our work, and how does the promise of eternal value address that temptation?

elements.text:
Rest and hope must be practised, not just believed. Some practices that nurture rest include:
  • Sabbath rhythm — a regular day for worship, joy, and renewal
  • Boundaries — stop when it’s time; trust God with the unfinished
  • Gratitude — end each day naming three good things from your work
  • Dependence — pray before tasks, not only after crises

elements.question: Which of these rhythms could you start this week?
What obstacles keep you from resting well?

elements.text: A Heart Check

elements.instruction: Rate yourself on each statement (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).
statements: I struggle to rest because I feel guilty when I’m not working.
I believe God is at work even when I’m not.
My hope in the new creation shapes how I view my work.
I often feel that my labour is ’in vain.’
I want to work and rest in dependence on Jesus.

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: How can your pattern of work and rest bear witness to your trust in God’s sovereignty?
What spiritual or physical habits could restore joy in your work?
How could your hope in the new creation help others find hope in their own struggles at work?

elements.text: Closing Prayer

Lord of creation and rest, you made the world good and called us to work within it. But our labour is hard, and we are weary. Thank you for the rest you offer in Jesus — rest for our souls now, and perfect rest to come in your renewed creation. Teach us to trust you enough to stop, to find joy in what you have done, and to work in hope until all things are made new. Amen.

Michael is a 32-year-old architect. He loves his job — the creativity, the challenge, the satisfaction of seeing his designs take shape.

But he also fears failure. When projects go well, he feels confident and alive. When they don’t, he feels worthless. He says:
“If I’m not doing well at work, I don’t know who I am any more. I know I’m supposed to find my identity in Christ, but it’s so hard when my job feels like me.”
Michael’s struggle raises a question every Christian worker must face: Who am I when the work stops? Or when it goes wrong?

elements.question: How much of your sense of worth comes from what you do?
When have you felt either proud or ashamed of your work performance?
What would change if your identity were completely secure in Christ?

elements.text: Before God gave Adam any tasks, he gave him identity. Adam’s worth came not from productivity, but from being made in God’s image. That truth is still the foundation of our dignity today.

elements.question: How does being created in the image of God shape the way we should see ourselves, and others, at work?
Why is it good news that your value comes before your performance?

elements.text: Sin twists work from service to self-definition. We ask our careers to tell us who we are. But in Christ, our identity is given, not achieved. We are God’s workmanship. Our works flow out of that new identity.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>For by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God; <sup>9</sup>it is not from works, so that no one can boast. <sup>10</sup>For we are his workmanship, having been created in Christ Jesus for good works that God prepared beforehand so we may do them.

elements.question: How does grace free you from defining yourself by your success?
elements.sampleAnswer: Grace means my standing before God is entirely a gift — not something I earned or could lose through failure. If I am saved by grace through faith and not by works, then my work cannot be the foundation of my identity. Success at work can be enjoyed as a gift and a means of serving others; failure can be faced with grief but not with existential collapse. Grace creates a stable platform that work cannot provide: I am loved and accepted before I have done anything today, and I will still be loved if today goes badly.
elements.questionHint: Paul says salvation is ’not from works, so that no one can boast’ — if work cannot contribute to what makes us acceptable before God, what does that tell us about whether work failure can take that acceptance away?

elements.question: How might remembering that you are God’s workmanship change the way you face failure or frustration?
elements.sampleAnswer: If I am God’s workmanship — his poem, his crafted work — then my value is not self-constructed and therefore cannot be self-destroyed. A failed project does not unmake me. A critical review does not define me. Frustration and setback are real experiences, but they do not reach the level where my identity is held. Remembering I am God’s workmanship also shifts my perspective on failure: perhaps what looks like a waste from my vantage point is part of the shaping process of a craftsman who sees the whole.
elements.questionHint: Paul calls us God’s ’workmanship’ — a word that speaks of something carefully made by a skilled craftsman. What does it mean for your sense of worth that you are something God made, rather than something you made of yourself?

elements.text: Comparison poisons contentment. Jesus calls us to follow him faithfully, not competitively. Faithfulness matters more than fame.

elements.passageText: <sup>20</sup>Peter turned around and saw the disciple whom Jesus loved following them. (This was the disciple who had leaned back against Jesus’ chest at the meal and asked, “Lord, who is the one who is going to betrayal you?”) <sup>21</sup>So when Peter saw him, he asked Jesus, “Lord, what about him?” <sup>22</sup>Jesus replied, “If I want him to live until I come back, what concern is that of yours? You follow me!“

elements.question: How does Jesus’ response challenge the culture of competition?
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter has just received a personal commission from Jesus — and immediately looks sideways at someone else. Jesus’ reply is almost abrupt in its directness: ‘What concern is that of yours? You follow me.’ The call is not to compare trajectories but to attend to your own. Comparison assumes that another person’s path is the standard by which yours should be measured. Jesus rejects that framework entirely. Each person has a calling given directly by him; comparing it to someone else’s is a distraction from the only question that matters: ‘Am I following Jesus faithfully in the path he has set for me?’
elements.questionHint: Peter has just been given his own clear calling by Jesus, and immediately asks about someone else’s — what does Jesus’ sharp response tell you about how much energy we should invest in comparing our path to others’?

elements.question: In what ways do you compare yourself with others at work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Comparison at work can be subtle: noticing who gets praised in meetings, tracking whose salary seems higher, measuring my output against a colleague’s, feeling diminished when someone else is promoted, or conversely feeling superior when I believe I am doing better than others. The trigger for Peter’s comparison was seeing John following Jesus — even a beautiful sight became an occasion for competitive anxiety. Jesus’ word to me is the same as to Peter: ‘You follow me.’ The path he has given me is mine; it is not in competition with anyone else’s.
elements.questionHint: Peter’s comparison began with a look over his shoulder at someone else — where in your working life do you find yourself most tempted to look sideways at others, and what does that comparison tend to produce in you?

elements.text: Jesus, the Lord of all, became a worker — a carpenter, a servant, a sufferer. His humility transforms how we think about ’low’ or ’high’ jobs. There are no insignificant workers when Christ is our model.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>You should have the same attitude toward one another that Christ Jesus had, <sup>6</sup>who though he existed in the form of God did not regard equality with God as something to be grasped, <sup>7</sup>but emptied himself by taking on the form of a slave, by looking like other men, and by sharing in human nature. <sup>8</sup>He humbled himself, by becoming obedient to the point of death – even death on a cross!

elements.question: How does Jesus’ humility shape your attitude to your work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus, who had every right to the highest possible status, took on the form of a slave and became obedient to the point of death. If the Son of God was willing to work in the most humbling conditions imaginable, no legitimate work is beneath the dignity of his followers. His humility also reframes ambition: rather than grasping for status, we are called to hold it loosely and serve from whatever position we occupy. The attitude Paul calls us to have ’toward one another’ is the one Christ modelled — which means every colleague, regardless of rank, deserves to be treated with the same regard Christ showed to us.
elements.questionHint: Paul says Jesus ’did not regard equality with God as something to be grasped’ — how might holding your own status, title, or position less tightly free you to serve others more generously?

elements.question: How could you honour the work of others more intentionally?
elements.sampleAnswer: Christ honoured human work by taking it on himself — the Word became flesh and worked with his hands. I can honour others’ work by: naming and thanking the people whose labour often goes unseen (maintenance staff, administrative colleagues, those in junior roles); not taking credit for collaborative work; acknowledging difficulty and effort rather than only celebrating results; asking people about their work with genuine interest; and treating someone’s contribution as valuable even when their role is lower in the hierarchy. Honouring is an active practice, not just an absence of contempt.
elements.questionHint: Christ honoured human nature by taking it on himself — whose work in your context goes most unnoticed or undervalued, and what would it look like to actively honour it?

elements.text: The gospel doesn’t erase ambition — it redeems it. We can work wholeheartedly for God’s glory, not self-glory. True ambition isn’t to be noticed, but to be faithful.

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>Slaves, obey your earthly masters in every respect, not only when they are watching – like those who are strictly people-pleasers – but with a sincere heart, fearing the Lord. <sup>23</sup>Whatever you are doing, work at it with enthusiasm, as to the Lord and not for people, <sup>24</sup>because you know that you will receive your inheritance from the Lord as the reward. Serve the Lord Christ.

elements.question: What’s the difference between godly and selfish ambition?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul distinguishes working ’as to the Lord’ from working as ’people-pleasers’. Selfish ambition is driven by the desire to be seen, praised, promoted, and rewarded by others — the audience is human. Godly ambition is equally energetic (‘work at it with enthusiasm’) but its driver is different: it seeks to please Christ, not impress people, and its reward is the inheritance God gives rather than the recognition humans offer. Selfish ambition is competitive, anxious, and deflated by others’ success. Godly ambition can rejoice in others’ flourishing because it is not running the same race.
elements.questionHint: Paul contrasts working ’as to the Lord’ with working only when someone is watching — what does that contrast reveal about who your ambition is really serving, and how you can tell the difference in practice?

elements.question: How could you direct your ambition toward service and worship?
elements.sampleAnswer: Directing ambition toward service means asking different questions about success: not ’how does this make me look?’ but ’how does this help the people I serve?’ Not ’will this get me noticed?’ but ’is this done with the quality and integrity that honours Christ?’ It might mean channelling the drive to excel into work that benefits others more visibly, or into excellence in an unglamorous role that few will notice but many will benefit from. As worship, it begins with the daily act of offering the day’s work to God before it starts, and returning it to him when it ends.
elements.questionHint: Paul says the reward for faithful work comes from ’the Lord’ rather than from people — how might working for that kind of reward, rather than human recognition, redirect where your energy and effort actually go?

elements.question: How can you tell when your desire for excellence has turned into pride?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s contrast between ’people-pleasers’ and those serving ’the Lord’ gives a useful diagnostic: ask who I need to impress in order to feel satisfied. If I can only feel good about my work when others praise it, that is a sign that the audience has shifted from God to people — and pride has entered. Another signal is how I respond to criticism or being overlooked: if my work being ignored produces disproportionate hurt or resentment, I may be asking it to do more identity work than it should. Excellence motivated by love is pleased when the work serves well; pride needs the work to reflect well on self.
elements.questionHint: Paul warns against working only when someone is watching — what does your reaction tell you when excellent work you have done goes unnoticed or uncredited?

elements.text: Identity in Christ brings rest. We still work hard, but not anxiously. We rest in the truth that God’s love doesn’t depend on our success.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Unless the Lord builds a house, those who build it labor in vain. Unless the Lord watches over a city, the watchman stays alert in vain. <sup>2</sup>It is vain for you to rise early, to stay up late, to toil hard for your food – yes, he provides for his beloved even when they sleep.

elements.question: How can remembering God’s sovereignty bring freedom?
elements.sampleAnswer: The psalm cuts the root of anxious overwork: unless God builds, the builders labour in vain. This is not a passive excuse to stop working, but a declaration that our effort operates within God’s sovereign governance — not outside it. If God is the ultimate builder, then my frantic extra hours are not what determines the outcome. That is enormously freeing: I work faithfully within my sphere, and trust God with the results beyond it. The closing image — God providing for his beloved while they sleep — is a picture of rest as confidence in his care, not just as physical recovery.
elements.questionHint: The psalm says it is ’vain’ to rise early and stay up late in anxious toil — but it does not say work is pointless. What is the specific thing the psalmist is calling vain, and what does that suggest about the difference between faithful work and anxious striving?

elements.question: What pressures drive you to overwork or anxiety?
elements.sampleAnswer: The psalmist identifies the temptation to rise early and stay up late — the compulsion to out-work the problem. The pressures behind this vary: financial insecurity, fear of failure or redundancy, the need for approval, perfectionism, or a deep discomfort with the feeling that things are beyond my control. Often the anxiety is not primarily about the work itself but about what failure would mean for my sense of self or security. The psalm locates the solution not in more effort but in remembering who actually governs the outcome.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist describes someone who rises early and stays up late in toil — what is the fear or pressure that drives that kind of compulsive overwork, and do you recognise any of it in yourself?

elements.question: What reminds you that you are loved even when you stop work?
elements.sampleAnswer: The psalm ends with the image of God providing for his beloved even while they sleep — love that is entirely independent of output. What reminds me of that love varies: the gospel itself, which says I am accepted in Christ before I have done anything today; the Sabbath rhythm, which practises stopping in trust; prayer, which orients me back to relationship rather than performance; and the people who love me for who I am rather than what I produce. The test of whether I believe God’s love is unconditional is partly whether I can stop and rest without guilt.
elements.questionHint: The psalm says God provides for his beloved ’even when they sleep’ — what practices or truths help you to actually experience that love as real when you are not working, rather than just believing it in theory?

elements.text: A Heart Check

elements.instruction: Rate yourself on each statement (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).
statements: My sense of worth depends on how successful I am at work.
I find it hard to accept failure or criticism.
I see myself first as a child of God, not by my job title.
I often compare my achievements with others’.
I want to work with humility and freedom in Christ.

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: What false identities do you tend to cling to in your work?
How could your church community help one another live out identity in Christ at work?
Who could you encourage this week to see their worth as rooted in God’s love, not their performance?

elements.text: Closing Prayer

Lord Jesus, you are the true image of God and the model worker. Thank you that my worth is not found in what I do, but in what you have done for me. Free me from the chains of comparison and pride. Teach me to work with joy, humility, and confidence in you. May my identity as your child shape every task I do, until all my labour becomes worship to your glory. Amen.

Anna has just finished university. She’s been offered two jobs. One pays well in a big city with lots of opportunity for advancement. The other is in her hometown: lower pay, slower pace, but closer to her church and family.
“I’ve prayed, I’ve made lists, I’ve asked for advice. I want to do what God wants, but how do I know what that is?”
Anna’s question is one we all ask at some point: How do I find work that is truly good — work that fits me, serves others, and glorifies God?

elements.question: Have you ever faced a difficult decision about your work or career path?
What criteria do you use when weighing such choices?
What role should prayer, wisdom, and community play in discerning your work?

elements.text: God rarely gives step-by-step instructions about our career choices. Instead, he shapes our hearts, builds our wisdom, and invites us to trust him as we walk forward. Finding good work is less about decoding secret signs and more about walking closely with a good Shepherd.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and do not rely on your own understanding. <sup>6</sup>Acknowledge him in all your ways, and he will make your paths straight.

elements.question: How should we approach decisions?
elements.sampleAnswer: The proverb gives a dual posture: wholehearted trust in God combined with a refusal to rely solely on our own analysis. This is not anti-rational — wisdom and careful thought are valued throughout Proverbs — but it places our own understanding in the right position: as a tool under God’s guidance, not as the final authority. ’Acknowledge him in all your ways’ suggests that the process of discernment should be characterised by ongoing reference to God — through prayer, through his word, through wise counsel — rather than a one-off consultation before reverting to self-reliance.
elements.questionHint: The proverb says to trust with ’all your heart’ and not rely on your ’own understanding’ — what is the difference between using your mind wisely in a decision and relying on your understanding in the way this verse warns against?

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>But if anyone is deficient in wisdom, he should ask God, who gives to all generously and without reprimand, and it will be given to him.

elements.question: What should we always do when seeking wisdom?
elements.sampleAnswer: James says to ask God — simply, directly, and with confidence. The remarkable thing is not just that God gives wisdom, but how he gives it: generously and without reprimand. He does not scold us for not knowing, or give grudgingly when asked. This means the first and most important step in any discernment process is prayer — not as a ritual to complete before getting on with things, but as a genuine asking of the God who wants to give. Many work decisions are made without this step ever really being taken.
elements.questionHint: James says God gives wisdom ’without reprimand’ to those who ask — what does that detail suggest about how God receives us when we come to him confused or uncertain about a decision?

elements.question: What does it mean to seek wisdom rather than certainty?
elements.sampleAnswer: Certainty would mean knowing in advance that a specific choice is definitely right. Wisdom is different: it is the capacity to make good judgments in the face of uncertainty, shaped by knowledge of God’s character, his word, and the input of wise people. Seeking wisdom rather than certainty means accepting that God may not give us a guaranteed sign, but he does give us the judgment we need to walk forward faithfully. It is a more mature and more trusting posture — less about eliminating risk, and more about developing the character and discernment to navigate it well.
elements.questionHint: James promises wisdom to those who ask, not certainty about outcomes — why might God choose to give wisdom rather than guaranteed answers, and what does that ask of us in return?

elements.text:
Good work is not defined by salary, status, or ease. It is defined by love, service, and faithfulness. Good work is marked by four things:
  • Serves others — it contributes to human flourishing
  • Fits your gifts — it uses what God has given you well
  • Aligns with God’s purposes — it honours his justice and truth
  • Can be done in faith — with a clear conscience before him

elements.passageText: <sup>31</sup>So whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do everything for the glory of God.

elements.question: Which of these four marks do you find hardest to achieve?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s standard — doing everything for God’s glory — is comprehensive and demanding. The hardest mark varies by person and circumstance. For some it is aligning work with God’s purposes when their industry operates in ethically grey areas. For others it is the fit between their gifts and their current role — they are capable but not fulfilled. For many, the hardest is simply doing everything with a clear conscience before God: honestly examining whether what they do and how they do it could genuinely be offered to him as an act of worship, or whether there are corners being cut and compromises being made that they prefer not to examine.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’whatever you do’ — which of the four marks feels most challenging for you specifically to meet in your current work situation, and what makes it difficult?

elements.question: How can we sum up what good work should look like?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s ’do everything for the glory of God’ is both simple and comprehensive. Good work is work that can honestly be offered to God: work that serves real human needs, is done with integrity and care, uses the gifts he has given, and can be carried out with a clear conscience. It is not defined by its prestige, pay, or visibility, but by whether it can be performed as an act of love for God and neighbour. The simplest test may be: could I genuinely say ’this is for you, Lord’ as I do it?
elements.questionHint: Paul’s summary is that everything should be done ’for the glory of God’ — what single question might you ask yourself about a piece of work or a job to test whether it meets that standard?

elements.question: Are there aspects of your current or desired work that don’t fit God’s purposes?
elements.sampleAnswer: Honest examination is required here. There may be aspects of a current role that are straightforwardly good, alongside practices or pressures that are harder to offer to God: pressure to mislead clients, systems that exploit vulnerable people, cultures of dishonesty or cruelty. Alternatively, a desired role may look attractive but involve serious compromise of integrity or alignment with what God values. Paul’s standard invites regular self-examination: not just ’is this legal?’ but ’can I do this for the glory of God?’
elements.questionHint: If you were to hold your current work up against Paul’s standard of ’everything for God’s glory’, what aspects would you find easy to offer to him — and what aspects would you hesitate to examine too closely?

elements.text: In Scripture, ‘calling’ is about belonging to Christ, not about our job. Our primary calling is to follow Jesus wherever we are. Our secondary callings (family, work, community) are contexts where we live that out.

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>Nevertheless, as the Lord has assigned to each one, as God has called each person, so must he live. I give this sort of direction in all the churches. <sup>18</sup>Was anyone called after he had been circumcised? He should not try to undo his circumcision. Was anyone called who is uncircumcised? He should not get circumcised. <sup>19</sup>Circumcision is nothing and uncircumcision is nothing. Instead, keeping God’s commandments is what counts. <sup>20</sup>Let each one remain in that situation in life in which he was called. <sup>21</sup>Were you called as a slave? Do not worry about it. But if indeed you are able to be free, make the most of the opportunity. <sup>22</sup>For the one who was called in the Lord as a slave is the Lord’s freedman. In the same way, the one who was called as a free person is Christ’s slave. <sup>23</sup>You were bought with a price. Do not become slaves of men. <sup>24</sup>In whatever situation someone was called, brothers and sisters, let him remain in it with God.

elements.question: How does the Bible challenge the idea of a ’perfect job’?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s repeated instruction to ’remain in the situation in which you were called’ cuts across the modern assumption that fulfilment depends on finding the ideal role. He is not saying never change jobs — he explicitly allows freedom to be pursued if available — but he is insisting that our primary calling is to Christ, not to a career. That means the situation we are currently in is already a valid context for faithful Christian living. The ’perfect job’ fantasy often assumes that meaning is elsewhere; Paul assumes it is available here, now, in whatever God has placed us.
elements.questionHint: Paul says to ’remain with God’ in whatever situation you are called to — what does the phrase ’with God’ add to the idea of staying in your current situation, and how does that change what it means to be where you are?

elements.question: How can you serve Christ faithfully in your current context?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s phrase ’remain in it with God’ is the key: the current context becomes an arena of faithfulness when God is present and active within it. Serving Christ faithfully in my current context might mean: bringing honesty and care to tasks I find routine; treating difficult colleagues with patience; looking for where I can contribute to flourishing in ways that go beyond my job description; praying regularly for the people around me; or simply doing excellent work as an offering to God. The question is not ’is this the right job?’ but ’am I following Christ here?’
elements.questionHint: Paul grounds his instruction in the phrase ’as the Lord has assigned’ — what might it mean to treat your current work situation as something God has placed you in, rather than something you are stuck in waiting to escape?

elements.text: God gives diverse gifts for the good of his body and the world. Our skills, experiences, and interests are not random — they’re part of his design. Good work often lies where our gifts meet the world’s needs.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>For by the grace given to me I say to every one of you not to think more highly of yourself than you ought to think, but to think with sober discernment, as God has distributed to each of you a measure of faith. <sup>4</sup>For just as in one body we have many members, and not all the members serve the same function, <sup>5</sup>so we who are many are one body in Christ, and individually we are members who belong to one another. <sup>6</sup>And we have different gifts according to the grace given to us. If the gift is prophecy, that individual must use it in proportion to his faith. <sup>7</sup>If it is service, he must serve; if it is teaching, he must teach; <sup>8</sup>if it is exhortation, he must exhort; if it is contributing, he must do so with sincerity; if it is leadership, he must do so with diligence; if it is showing mercy, he must do so with cheerfulness.

elements.question: Why is it foolish to expect to be the same as everyone else?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul uses the body metaphor to make the point unavoidable: a body where every part was the same would be no body at all — it would be dysfunctional. God has deliberately distributed different gifts ’according to his grace’, which means diversity is not a problem to be resolved but a design feature to be honoured. Expecting everyone to have the same gifts, the same strengths, or the same calling is to misunderstand how God has constructed his people. It also leads to two errors: either thinking you are better than others because your gift is more visible, or thinking you are worse because yours is less so.
elements.questionHint: Paul says gifts are distributed ’according to grace’ — what does it mean that your particular gifts are a matter of grace rather than achievement, and how does that change how you should regard both what you have and what you lack?

elements.question: What particular gifts or abilities has God entrusted to you?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s list covers a wide range — prophecy, service, teaching, exhortation, contributing, leadership, mercy — and the variety is deliberate. Each person has a particular combination that is theirs to steward. Identifying mine requires both honest self-reflection and listening to others, since we often undervalue gifts that come naturally to us. The test Paul implies is not ’what do I feel passionate about?’ but ’what has God graciously given me to use for the body’s good?’ — a gift-centred rather than purely desire-centred approach to discernment.
elements.questionHint: Paul names very different gifts and says each person has been given a measure — who in your life has been able to see and name gifts in you that you might have difficulty seeing in yourself?

elements.question: What kinds of work energise you? What kinds drain you? (What might that reveal?)
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says the one who shows mercy should do it ’with cheerfulness’ and the one who leads should do it ’with diligence’ — there is an alignment between gift and disposition. Work that consistently energises us often (though not always) aligns with the gifts God has given us: it draws on what he built into us. Work that consistently drains us may indicate a poor fit between the role and our gifts — or it may simply be the cost of serving in a fallen world. The distinction matters: some depletion is normal, but chronic exhaustion without fruit is worth examining honestly.
elements.questionHint: Paul describes different gifts as naturally suited to different people — what might the pattern of what energises and depletes you in your work be revealing about where your particular gifts lie?

elements.text: Work decisions should rarely be made alone. The church community is a family that helps one another discern, test, and celebrate God’s leading. Wise counsel protects us from self-deception and fear.

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>Plans fail when there is no counsel, but with abundant advisers they are established.

elements.question: Who do you trust to help you put Proverbs into practice as you discern work decisions?
elements.sampleAnswer: Proverbs values ’abundant advisers’ — not just one voice, but a plurality of wise perspectives. Good counsellors for work decisions are people who know me well enough to spot self-deception, who are mature in faith and wisdom, who will tell me the truth rather than just what I want to hear, and who have relevant experience or insight. They might include a pastor, a mentor, a trusted friend who knows my working life, and perhaps people who have experience in the field I am considering. The proverb implies that seeking counsel is not a sign of weakness but of wisdom.
elements.questionHint: The proverb uses the word ’abundant’ for advisers — not just one person, but many. Who are the different people in your life whose counsel about work decisions you could seek, and what might each of them see that others might miss?

elements.text: Sometimes both options seem good, or neither does. Remember: God’s will is not a tightrope to balance on in fear, but a wide field to walk in grace. If your heart’s desire is to honour him, you can go forward in freedom.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>Trust in the Lord and do what is right! Settle in the land and maintain your integrity! <sup>4</sup>Then you will take delight in the Lord, and he will answer your prayers. <sup>5</sup>Commit your future to the Lord! Trust in him, and he will act on your behalf.

elements.question: How can delighting in God shape your desires and decisions?
elements.sampleAnswer: The psalmist says that when we delight in the Lord, he gives us the desires of our heart — which does not mean he grants every wish, but that he shapes our desires so they align with his purposes. The more deeply I know and enjoy God, the more my wants begin to reflect what he values: service, integrity, love, faithfulness. This means that good work discernment is not primarily an analytical exercise but a relational one — the closer I am to God, the more my desires become trustworthy guides rather than things I have to override or distrust.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist says to ’delight in the Lord’ and then promises he will give you the desires of your heart — what does that sequence suggest about the relationship between closeness to God and the reliability of our own desires?

elements.question: What fears keep you from making decisions with freedom?
elements.sampleAnswer: The psalmist calls us to commit our future to the Lord and trust that he will act — an invitation to let go of the control we cannot actually exercise. Common fears that prevent free decision-making include: fear of making the wrong choice and missing God’s will; fear of financial insecurity; fear of others’ disapproval; fear of failure in the new role; and fear of the unknown. Many of these fears assume that everything depends on getting the decision exactly right. The psalm invites a different posture: commit, trust, and let God act — which is only possible if we genuinely believe he can be trusted with the outcome.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist says to ’commit your future to the Lord’ — what specific fear about your own future would you need to release in order to make that commitment genuinely, rather than just as a formula?

elements.text: A Heart Check

elements.instruction: Rate yourself on each statement (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).
statements: I believe there is only one ’right’ job God wants me to find.
I trust God to guide me even when I can’t see the whole path.
I seek wise counsel from mature Christians about my work decisions.
I feel free to serve Christ faithfully wherever I am.
I want my future work to express love for God and neighbour.

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: How would you describe your sense of calling in one sentence?
What would ’good work’ look like for you in this season of life?
How could your church help members discern and support one another’s vocations?

elements.text: Closing Prayer

Faithful God, you are the Lord of our lives and the giver of every calling. Thank you that you lead not by fear but by wisdom and grace. Teach us to trust you with our choices and our future. Help us to delight in you so that our desires align with yours.

May every task we choose and every skill we use bring honour to you and good to our neighbour. Amen.

intro: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each chapter — both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have wrestled personally with the questions before they help others do the same.

Start with the case study. Each session opens with a realistic scenario. Resist the urge to rush past it to the Bible questions. The case study is designed to surface real feelings and honest questions before the teaching begins — allow people to identify with it.

Create a safe space for honesty. Many Christians carry unspoken shame or confusion about their work — feeling their job is too ordinary, too secular, or too compromised. Your job is to make the group feel safe enough to say what they actually think, not what they think a Christian is supposed to say.

Don’t rush the silences. Some of the best moments in a group study happen after a question lands and nobody speaks for a few seconds. Resist the urge to fill every silence — especially in the Heart Check exercises.
Watch for the person who goes quiet. Sometimes the most significant thing happening in the room is with the person who says the least. Check in with them afterwards.

chapters.title: When Work Feels Empty
chapters.keyPoints:
This session establishes the theological framework for the whole series: work is part of God’s good creation (Genesis 2), distorted by sin (Genesis 3), but redeemed in Christ (Colossians 3:23–24). All eight sessions build on this foundation.

The key move is from seeing work as a spiritually neutral necessity to seeing it as a potential act of worship and love. David’s story (financial officer feeling his work ‘doesn’t count for Jesus’) will resonate widely — including with people in ministry roles who are just as prone to misplacing their identity.

The Ephesians 2:8–10 passage is crucial: we do not work in order to become someone; we work out of who we already are in Christ. Ground the whole session in that identity.
chapters.pastorals:
Be sensitive to group members who may be in genuinely difficult or unfulfilling work situations. The aim of this session is not to make people feel guilty for not ‘worshipping enough’ at their desks, but to reframe the question entirely: who are you before you start work?

Some people feel trapped in work they did not choose or cannot leave. Do not push applications that assume free career choice. Focus instead on identity and attitude rather than circumstance.
chapters.watch: Watch for the person who has been secretly considering leaving their job for ‘full-time ministry’. This session directly addresses that impulse — gently, not dismissively. Make sure the group hears that ordinary work matters to God, without implying that vocational ministry does not.

chapters.title: Work as Love Made Visible
chapters.keyPoints:
The central claim of this session is that work is one of the primary ways we love others. The helpful distinction is between loving many people in a few ways (through work tasks and skills) and loving a few people in many ways (through personal relationships).

Draw out the implication of Genesis 1:26 — that we are made in the image of a relational, collaborative God, and that work is therefore inherently relational and not merely productive.

Mark 10:42–45 redefines greatness as service. This challenges both the ambition that drives people to overwork and the false humility that leads people to dismiss their work as too small to matter.
chapters.pastorals:
Judith’s story (stay-at-home parent feeling invisible) is important. Make sure unpaid and domestic work is explicitly affirmed as real work throughout this session. Some of the most significant loving-through-work happens in contexts that receive no economic recognition.

Ben’s story (IT worker feeling disconnected from ministry) is common. The question ‘who benefits from your work?’ can be surprisingly powerful for people in technical or support roles who have never mapped their own impact.
chapters.watch: Watch for people who confuse ‘loving others through work’ with ‘evangelising at work’. Both matter, but they are not the same thing. This session is about the intrinsic value of the work itself as love — not only its instrumental value as an opportunity for gospel witness.

chapters.title: Work is Good in Itself
chapters.keyPoints:
This session deepens the creation theology of Chapter 1. Work is not merely permitted by God; it is part of his design for human flourishing. Daniel’s story (‘just making boxes’) gives the group permission to voice the nagging sense that their work is too ordinary to matter.

The important move is from three categories of work: work that reflects creation (cultivating, building, teaching), work that responds to the Fall (healing, restraining evil, repairing), and work that points to redemption (proclaiming, showing mercy). All three glorify God when done faithfully — and most real jobs contain elements of more than one.

Romans 12:1 and the ‘living sacrifice’ language is the theological anchor: all of life — including work — can be offered to God as worship.
chapters.pastorals:
People in unglamorous, repetitive, or low-status work particularly need this session. Be concrete about examples: a factory worker, a cleaner, a data entry clerk. The gospel’s answer to ‘I’m just making boxes’ is not ‘find a more spiritual job’ but ‘this is how God sustains his world, and you are part of it.’

The Revelation 21 passage can feel abstract. Help the group connect the hope of new creation to their actual work: the skills, creativity, and care invested in faithful work are not wasted — they have a kind of eternal weight, even if we do not understand exactly how.
chapters.watch: Watch for the temptation to spiritualise work in a way that devalues its actual content. The point is not that making boxes becomes spiritual by adding a Bible verse to it — it is that the making of useful things for human life is already a participation in God’s care for his world.

chapters.title: The Fruit of Work
chapters.keyPoints:
This session tackles the fruit of work: income, influence, skill, and relationships — and what we do with them. Lydia’s story introduces the subtle danger that success can pull us away from God just as surely as failure can.

The key theological moves are: (1) all fruit ultimately comes from God’s provision (Deuteronomy 8:17–18); (2) wealth is stored work, designed to serve love rather than self-protection (1 Timothy 6:17–19); (3) generosity is not optional but the natural overflow of knowing God’s grace (2 Corinthians 8–9).

The four types of fruit — provision, service, formation, witness — give the group a richer vocabulary than simply ‘salary’. Encourage people to think about all four in relation to their own work.
chapters.pastorals:
Be sensitive to those in genuine financial difficulty. The application of generosity must be handled carefully — it is not about giving from scarcity in a way that damages families, but about the orientation of the heart.

The Luke 12:15–21 parable (the rich fool) can land hard on high earners. Handle it honestly but not as an attack. The question Jesus presses is not ‘how much do you earn?’ but ‘what does your life consist of?’ — which is as searching for the poor as for the wealthy.
chapters.watch: Watch for group members who equate material success with spiritual blessing, and those who equate material struggle with spiritual failure. Both tendencies distort the gospel. The session steers between them by grounding all fruit in grace, not merit.

chapters.title: The Context of Work
chapters.keyPoints:
Work is never done in isolation — it is always embedded in systems, organisations, and power structures. Samuel’s story (the accountant facing pressure to stay silent about financial manipulation) raises the question of ethical integrity in complex organisations.

Daniel 6 is the key case study: a believer who served faithfully within a pagan system, maintained his integrity at personal cost, and refused to allow the system to define his loyalties. He worked within a broken system without being controlled by it.

Jeremiah 29:4–7 — ‘seek the welfare of the city’ — provides a positive vision: we are not called merely to survive our workplaces but to bless them.
chapters.pastorals:
This session may surface significant pain. Some group members may be in genuinely unjust or compromised workplaces and feel trapped. Be careful not to imply that staying in a difficult context is always right, or that leaving is always a failure of nerve.

The question of when to speak up, when to stay, and when to step away (Psalm 15:1–5) requires wisdom that cannot be given in the abstract. If someone is facing a specific ethical dilemma, offer to speak with them separately rather than trying to resolve it in the group discussion.
chapters.watch: Watch for two opposite errors: the person who is too quick to separate from a difficult workplace (leaving rather than being salt and light), and the person who has rationalised going along with something genuinely wrong. Both need this session, but for different reasons.

chapters.title: Work and Hope
chapters.keyPoints:
Naomi’s story (the exhausted nurse) introduces the theme of work and weariness — and the urgent need for a theology of rest. The session connects rest, Sabbath, and the hope of new creation.

Three theological anchors: (1) God built rest into creation before sin (Genesis 2:1–3) — rest is not laziness but an imitation of God and an act of trust; (2) the Sabbath connects our identity as God’s people, not slaves (Exodus 20 and Deuteronomy 5); (3) 1 Corinthians 15:58 grounds present faithfulness in resurrection hope — our work is not in vain.

Matthew 11:28–30 is the pastoral heart of the session. Jesus does not promise less work — he promises a new way of working, with him, under his yoke.
chapters.pastorals:
Be alert to genuine burnout in the group. This session is not primarily a call to try harder to rest — it is a call to receive rest as a gift and to trust God enough to stop. For someone running on empty, the most important thing may simply be to hear that their exhaustion is seen and taken seriously.

The four practical practices at the end of the session (Sabbath rhythm, boundaries, gratitude, dependence) are suggestions, not a programme. Help the group identify one concrete step rather than feeling overwhelmed by a list.
chapters.watch: Watch for the person who cannot rest — whose overwork has become a form of self-justification or control. For them, Sabbath is not a minor lifestyle adjustment but a profound act of trust. The session’s answer to their anxiety is the resurrection: Jesus rose, so our work — however unfinished — is held in his hands.

chapters.title: The Worker
chapters.keyPoints:
This session turns from the nature and context of work to the identity of the worker. Michael’s story (the architect whose worth rises and falls with project outcomes) names a dynamic that affects almost everyone in the group, whatever they do.

The key theological move is from Ephesians 2:8–10: we are God’s workmanship — our identity is given, not achieved. We do not work to become someone; we work because of who we already are in Christ.

Philippians 2:5–8 provides the counter-model: Jesus, who had every right to status, voluntarily took the form of a servant. True greatness — and true freedom from the anxiety of comparison — is found in following his pattern.
chapters.pastorals:
This session may feel uncomfortably close to home for high-achievers in the group. The language of idolatry (‘our careers tell us who we are’) can feel accusatory. Be careful to name it as a universal tendency, not a personal failure.

Psalm 127:1–2 is a significant comfort: the Father provides for his beloved even while they sleep. This is not an invitation to laziness but a profound statement about where our security lies. Some in the group may need to hear this more than anything else.
chapters.watch: Watch for the person who intellectually affirms identity in Christ but emotionally still measures themselves by their performance. Understanding the gospel and being formed by it are different things. The Heart Check questions at the end of this session are designed to expose that gap — make sure the group takes them seriously.

chapters.title: Finding Good Work
chapters.keyPoints:
The final session brings the series to a practical conclusion: how do Christians discern and choose good work? Anna’s dilemma (two jobs, no obvious ‘right answer’) is deliberately unresolved — because the point is not that God always provides a clear signal, but that he provides wisdom, community, and freedom.

Four marks of good work: it serves others, fits your gifts, aligns with God’s purposes, and can be done in faith. These are not a checklist for a perfect job — they are a framework for faithful discernment.

1 Corinthians 7:17–24 corrects the idea that our primary calling is to find the right job. Our primary calling is to follow Jesus wherever we are. Secondary callings (work, family, community) are contexts for that primary calling, not replacements for it.
chapters.pastorals:
Be sensitive to people who feel stuck — in the wrong career, the wrong organisation, or with no clear path forward. The session’s message is one of freedom (‘God’s will is a wide field, not a tightrope’) but that freedom can feel hollow to someone who genuinely cannot see a way forward. Point them toward community and wise counsel, not just principles.

This is a good session to close by inviting the group to pray for one another’s specific work situations — naming their jobs, their decisions, and their struggles before God together.
chapters.watch: Watch for the person leaving the series more aware of the gap between where they are and where they want to be, but without a concrete next step. Help everyone identify one specific action: a conversation to have, a decision to revisit, a prayer to pray, or a person to meet with. The series should end with movement, not just insight.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).

studyAiData.aiMentorPrompt: You are a warm, thoughtful mentor for a Bible study series called ‘Working for the Good: Faith, Love, and Worship in our Everyday Labour’ — An eight-session Bible study exploring what it means to work faithfully as a Christian. Drawing on passages from Genesis to Revelation, each session tackles a different dimension of work: its God-given goodness, its distortion by sin, and its redemption in Christ. Whether you feel your job is meaningless, disconnected from your faith, or simply exhausting, this study invites you to see your daily labour as an act of love, a form of worship, and part of God’s larger purposes for the world.
